| cp01_entry | ✅/❌      | ✅/❌        |       |

## Validator Result
- Status: PASS / PASS-with-warnings / FAIL
- Warnings:
- Issues:

## Next Improvement
<!-- One concrete change for the next run -->

05.title: G1 Humanoid Communication & Safety Workflow
05.thesis: Day 5 establishes the G1 as a peer-to-peer DDS system where every communication lane carries a distinct risk profile. Students build custom Publisher/Subscriber classes with explicit QoS policies, wire API registration through ID-based service discovery, and layer three independent safety mechanisms — watchdog heartbeats, smooth deceleration profiles, and hardware emergency stops — before any motion command is issued.
rules: Map every G1 operation to its communication lane and risk level: subscribe = passive observation (low risk), publish = active command (medium risk), RPC = service request (medium risk). Never confuse these lanes — a publish looks like a subscribe at the socket level but carries fundamentally different authority.

Custom DDS classes must declare QoS explicitly: RELIABILITY (RELIABLE vs BEST_EFFORT), DURABILITY (VOLATILE vs TRANSIENT_LOCAL), HISTORY (KEEP_LAST with depth), and DEADLINE (missed deadline = safety event). Build the class, register its QoS, then verify with a dry-run subscriber before any live topic.

Layer safety mechanisms so no single failure produces an unsafe state: (1) watchdog heartbeats detect process death and trigger automatic damp, (2) smooth deceleration profiles bound jerk and reject instantaneous velocity discontinuities, (3) hardware emergency stops bypass software entirely and cut motor power at the physical layer.

pacing.session: Day 5 Position in Course & G1 Hardware Introduction
G1 Communication Model: Subscribe / Publish / RPC
Lab 2 — Read-Only FSM Inspection & Readiness States
G1 vs Go2/B2 Transfer, Troubleshooting & Assessment
Knowledge Check, Lab Record Completion & Day 6 Preview

slides.title: Day 5: G1 Humanoid Communication and Safety Workflow
slides.thesis: Day 5 establishes the critical aspects of G1 humanoid robotics: communication architecture and safety workflow. Students build foundational knowledge for interacting with and ensuring safe operation of G1 humanoids.

board_data.label: Understand G1
board_data.value: Subsystems, bipedal mechanics, hardware configurations, actuator/sensor roles, balance assumptions, and safe operating boundaries.

board_data.label: Initialize DDS
board_data.value: Peer-to-peer topology, topic structures, message flow, discovery protocol, initialization configuration, and communication verification.

board_data.label: Register API
board_data.value: API definition and registration, custom publisher/subscriber classes with explicit QoS, message handler organization.

board_data.label: Safety Loops
board_data.value: Watchdog heartbeats, smooth deceleration profiles, hardware emergency stops — layered, independent, no shared fate.

slides.bottom_band: Safety First: Day 5 integrates safety considerations at every step. The robot is observed, not commanded. Observability is the foundation of safe operation.

slides.title: Learning Map — Path to G1 Mastery
slides.thesis: Today's journey progresses through five stages: understand G1 platform, initialize DDS communication, register APIs and build custom classes, implement safety loops and watchdogs, and validate with evidence and pass readiness gates.
board_data: Understand G1 — Platform overview: subsystems, bipedal mechanics, hardware variants, actuator/sensor roles, balance assumptions, beginner-safe operating boundaries.
Initialize DDS — Communication backbone: peer-to-peer topology, topic structures, initialization configurations, message flow, discovery protocol, communication verification tools.

Register APIs & Build Custom Classes — Programmatic interface: API registration, custom publisher classes (DataWriter + QoS), custom subscriber classes (DataReader + callbacks), message handler organization, command/feedback validation.
Implement Safety Loops — Layered protection: structural safety loops, real-time fallback watchdogs, controlled stop behavior, emergency termination logic, software termination scripts.
Validate & Pass Readiness Gates — Evidence-driven: lab validation, evidence checklist, readiness review, debugging habits, validation gates for deployment.
slides.bottom_band: Safety First: Our Guiding Principle — Safety is not an afterthought. Every step integrates safety considerations. Master observation before you ever command motion.

slides.title: G1 Humanoid Overview — Introduction
slides.thesis: The G1 humanoid is an advanced bipedal robot designed for complex human-like interactions. Its modular design means subsystems — sensors, actuators, control unit — work together for coordinated movement and sensing.
board_data: Advanced Bipedal Robot — Designed for complex human-like interactions, dynamic locomotion, and dexterous manipulation in human-scale environments.

Modular Design — Subsystems work together for coordinated movement and sensing: computing (Jetson Orin NX), perception (depth camera + LiDAR), actuation (23–43 DOF), and communication (DDS middleware).
Integrated System — Each component plays a vital role. A failure in one subsystem propagates through the DDS communication layer — which is why Day 5 starts with observability before actuation.
slides.bottom_band: Think of G1 as a highly integrated system where every component communicates through DDS. Understanding these interactions is key to effective and safe control.

slides.title: G1 Subsystem Interactions
slides.thesis: G1 subsystems are in constant communication: sensors feed data to the control unit, which commands actuators, which change the world, which sensors measure again. This closed-loop architecture enables dynamic and responsive behavior.

board_data.label: Sensors → Data
board_data.value: Provide real-time data: joint encoders (position, velocity), IMU (orientation, angular velocity), force/torque sensors (contact forces), depth camera + LiDAR (environmental mapping). All sensor data flows through DDS topics.

board_data.label: Control Unit → Decisions
board_data.value: Processes sensor data, runs locomotion controllers and FSM, makes decisions, and sends commands. The Jetson Orin NX executes control loops at 100 Hz–1 kHz.

board_data.label: Actuators → Action
board_data.value: Execute commands: joint motors for locomotion and manipulation, grippers for object interaction. Each actuator reports state back through DDS — closing the feedback loop.

slides.bottom_band: The sensor → control → actuator loop runs continuously. Understanding this data flow is prerequisite to debugging any communication failure.

slides.title: Bipedal Mechanics — The Challenge of Balance
slides.thesis: Bipedal balance is inherently challenging. Three fundamental concepts govern G1 stability: Center of Mass (CoM), Support Polygon, and Zero Moment Point (ZMP). Every locomotion command — even standing still — affects all three.

board_data.label: Center of Mass (CoM)
board_data.value: The weighted average position of the robot mass (~35 kg, roughly at torso when upright). Any arm or leg movement shifts the CoM — the controller must compensate continuously to maintain stability.

board_data.label: Support Polygon
board_data.value: The convex hull of ground contact points. Double support (both feet flat) provides the largest polygon. Single support (one foot) collapses it drastically — reducing the stable region and requiring active balance control.

board_data.label: Zero Moment Point (ZMP)
board_data.value: The point on the ground where total moment from ground reaction forces is zero. If ZMP stays inside the support polygon, the robot is dynamically stable. If it drifts outside, the robot tips — and must take a corrective step or fall.

slides.bottom_band: On Day 5, you observe these through lowstate IMU readings. A robot with ZMP near the support polygon edge is one disturbance away from a fall — even when standing still.

slides.title: Hardware Configurations — Understanding the Variants
slides.thesis: G1 humanoids ship in multiple hardware configurations. The arm DOF variant (arm5 = 23 total DOF, arm7 = 29 total DOF) changes motor_state array size, joint index ranges, and command dimensions — using the wrong variant in code causes dimension mismatches.

rows: Arm5 (Standard)
5 per arm
Simpler manipulation — smaller command arrays, fewer joint limits to check.
Arm7 (Extended)
7 per arm
Additional wrist/elbow DOF — finer manipulation, larger command arrays, more complex kinematics.
Standard Sensors
Varies
Varies
Varies
Depth camera + LiDAR baseline. Force/torque at ankles only.
Enhanced Sensors
Varies
Varies
Varies
Additional cameras, wrist force/torque sensors for richer perception and control.

slides.bottom_band: Confirm the DOF variant before writing any code that touches motor_state. The wrong variant produces command arrays of the wrong dimension, which is a safety hazard.

slides.title: Actuator and Sensor Roles
slides.thesis: Actuators are the muscles of G1 — motors for joints, grippers for manipulation. Sensors are the senses — encoders for position, IMUs for orientation, force sensors for interaction. On Day 5: observe sensors, do not command actuators.

board_data.label: Joint Motors (Actuators)
board_data.value: Brushless DC motors with harmonic drive reduction at each DOF. Accept position, velocity, or torque commands. On Day 5, motors are strictly observed via motor_state — not commanded.

board_data.label: Joint Encoders (Sensors)
board_data.value: Position encoders at every motor report angle in radians via rt/lowstate motor_state array. Verify: are all encoder values within expected joint limit ranges?

board_data.label: IMU (Sensor)
board_data.value: Inertial Measurement Unit reports orientation (quaternion), angular velocity (rad/s), and linear acceleration (m/s²). Check imu_state.quaternion in lowstate: is the robot upright?

board_data.label: Force/Torque Sensors (Sensors)
board_data.value: Six-axis F/T sensors at ankles (wrists on enhanced config). Critical for ZMP calculation and ground contact detection. Present in lowstate if equipped.

slides.bottom_band: The actuator/sensor separation defines Day 5's operator role: you are a sensor reader building observability skills. Commands come on Day 6 — after you have proven you can read the robot.

slides.title: Balance Assumptions in G1 Control
slides.thesis: G1's balance controller operates under explicit assumptions. Violating them — operating on a slope, pushing the robot, or adding unknown payload — produces unpredictable behavior. Know the assumptions before you operate.
board_data: Flat, Rigid Ground — Controller assumes a horizontal, non-deformable surface. Slopes, carpets, or soft mats invalidate the contact model and cause ZMP estimation errors.
Known Mass Distribution — CoM position is computed from a calibrated mass model. Adding payload, removing covers, or modifying hardware changes mass distribution and degrades balance.
Limited External Disturbances — Controller can reject small perturbations (light wind, minor bumps). It cannot handle strong pushes, collisions, or dynamic loading without risking a fall.
Adequate Foot Friction — Support polygon assumes sufficient friction to prevent slip. Wet, oily, or dusty floors reduce friction and can cause foot slip during posture transitions.
No Simultaneous Multi-Joint Saturation — If multiple motors hit torque limits simultaneously, the controller cannot redistribute forces and may enter an unrecoverable state.
slides.bottom_band: Every assumption is a potential failure mode. When diagnosing unexpected behavior, check assumptions before debugging code — the environment often changes before the software does.

slides.title: Beginner-Safe Operating Boundaries
slides.thesis: Day 5 operates within explicitly restricted boundaries: no motion commands, read-only observability, supervised environment with instructor present. These boundaries are the safety framework that enables learning without risk — not limitations on capability.

rows: No Motion Commands
Zero publish to motor control topics. Subscribe and read-only RPC (GET) only.
Prevents any accidental actuation during the learning phase.
Code review confirms no DataWriter for motor command topics.
Restricted Joint Observation
Read motor_state but do not interpret values as actionable commands.
Separates observation from action — critical habit formation.
All code reviews verify no publish() calls to motor topics.
Supervised Environment
Instructor present, exclusion zone marked, stop authority designated.
Human oversight catches errors before they become incidents.
Instructor sign-off required before each lab section.
Lab Record Discipline
Document: operator, interface, readings, classifications, timestamps.
Creates auditable evidence trail for all safety decisions.
Lab record reviewed and signed at end of each session.

slides.bottom_band: Beginner boundaries are progressive — Day 5 restrictions lift partially on Day 6 (gated motion) and further on Day 7 (integrated sequences). Each day earns its privileges through demonstrated competence.

slides.title: Advanced Bipedal Locomotion Concepts
slides.thesis: While Day 5 is no-motion, understanding G1's advanced locomotion concepts helps interpret sensor data: gait generation creates rhythmic motion, footstep planning navigates environments, and disturbance rejection maintains balance against external forces.

board_data.label: Gait Generation
board_data.value: Algorithms produce rhythmic joint trajectories alternating single and double support phases. In lowstate, watch mode_machine transition through gait-related FSM states — even when stationary.

board_data.label: Footstep Planning
board_data.value: Computes where each foot should land given desired velocity and terrain. The planner runs continuously, waiting for a velocity command. Observed in lowstate as computed footstep targets.

board_data.label: Disturbance Rejection
board_data.value: Active compensation for external forces. Visible in lowstate as rapid motor_state adjustments even when standing still — the controller is constantly balancing against micro-perturbations.

slides.title: Sensor Fusion for Perception
slides.thesis: G1 fuses data from multiple sensors to build a unified state estimate. No single sensor tells the full story — the fusion algorithm combines IMU, encoders, cameras, depth, and force data into one coherent, probabilistic picture.

rows: IMU + Joint Encoders
Absolute orientation (IMU) + relative joint positions (encoders) for full-body state.
Forward kinematics: know where every link is in world frame.
Depth Camera + LiDAR
Near-field depth (0.3–5 m) fused with far-field LiDAR for complete mapping.
3D occupancy grid for footstep planning and obstacle avoidance.
Force Sensors + IMU
Ground reaction forces + orientation = real-time ZMP computation.
Dynamic stability metric — is the ZMP inside the support polygon?
All Sensors → State Estimator
Kalman filter fusing all inputs into a single probabilistic state estimate.
One coherent state: pose, velocity, contact state, and uncertainty bounds.

slides.bottom_band: Sensor fusion is why lowstate contains coordinated, cross-validated data. When one sensor disagrees with others, the fusion flags an anomaly — visible as degraded state estimation quality in lowstate.

slides.title: G1 DDS Communications — Introduction
slides.thesis: Data Distribution Service (DDS) is G1's communication backbone: the standard for real-time, reliable, and scalable data exchange. It is decentralized (no central broker), discovers participants dynamically, and enforces QoS contracts per data stream.
board_data: Data Distribution Service (DDS) — The standard middleware for real-time, reliable, scalable data exchange. All G1 internal and external communication flows through DDS.
Decentralized Architecture — No central message broker. Publishers and subscribers discover each other directly on the network. If one node fails, others continue communicating unaffected.
Dynamic Discovery — Participants automatically find each other via multicast/unicast. No hardcoded IP addresses. A new subscriber receives existing topics immediately upon joining the domain.
Type-Safe Data Exchange — Every topic has a defined data type (IDL). Publisher and subscriber types must match exactly. Type mismatch = no communication — caught at discovery time, not runtime.
QoS-Contracted Delivery — Each DataWriter/DataReader pair negotiates Quality of Service: reliability, durability, history depth, deadline periods, and liveliness checks.
slides.bottom_band: DDS is not a black box. On Day 5, you verify DDS connectivity, inspect topic structures, and confirm message flow — these diagnostic skills separate safe operators from guessers.

slides.title: DDS Topic Structures
slides.thesis: In DDS, all communication happens through topics — named data streams, each dedicated to a specific type of information. Publishers send data to topics. Subscribers receive data from topics. The topic name is the contract.

rows: rt/lowstate
Subscribe (robot → operator)
LowState
tick, mode_machine, mode_pr, IMU quaternion/angular_velocity, motor_state[]
Low
rt/arm_sdk
Publish (operator → robot)
ArmCmd
Joint position/velocity/torque targets per arm DOF
High
rt/locomotion
Publish (operator → robot)
LocoCmd
Velocity commands (vx, vy, vyaw), posture transitions
High
api/*
RPC (operator ↔ robot)
Service-specific
API registration, mode switching, parameter get/set
Medium
rt/audio
Publish (operator → robot)
AudioCmd
Audio file playback, TTS text, volume control
Low

slides.bottom_band: The topic name encodes the risk level. rt/ topics carry real-time data. api/ topics are service calls. Learn the topic map before writing a single line of code.

slides.title: Communication Layout Topology — Peer-to-Peer
slides.thesis: DDS on G1 uses a pure peer-to-peer topology: every participant communicates directly with every other participant. No central router, no broker, no single point of failure. Components discover each other dynamically.

board_data.label: Peer-to-Peer Data Flow
board_data.value: Your subscriber receives data directly from the G1 onboard publisher — no intermediate hops. Minimizes latency but requires both peers on the same DDS domain and reachable network.

board_data.label: Dynamic Discovery Protocol
board_data.value: Participants announce via Simple Discovery Protocol (SDP). Within seconds of joining, a new participant knows about all existing DataWriters/DataReaders — and they know about it.

board_data.label: No Single Point of Failure
board_data.value: Operator workstation disconnects → onboard controller continues. Motor controller drops → other controllers continue. System degrades gracefully rather than failing entirely.

board_data.label: Domain Isolation
board_data.value: DDS domains (domain ID integer) isolate communication groups. G1 typically uses domain 0. Multiple G1s on same network must use different domain IDs to prevent cross-talk.

slides.bottom_band: Peer-to-peer means your workstation IS a DDS participant — not a remote terminal. A misconfigured publisher on your workstation can interfere with robot operation. This power demands discipline.

slides.title: DDS Initialization Configurations
slides.thesis: Before G1 components can communicate, DDS must be properly initialized: configuration files define the DDS domain, QoS policies, and participant roles. A single misconfigured parameter causes silent communication failure.
board_data: Configuration Files — XML or environment variables define transport settings, discovery parameters, and QoS profiles. G1 uses CycloneDDS with CYCLONEDDS_URI for config path.
Domain ID — Integer (typically 0) that isolates DDS traffic. All participants on the same domain see each other. Different domains are completely invisible.
ChannelFactoryInitialize(domain_id, interface_name) — Creates the local DDS participant bound to the specified network interface. This is the entry point for all DDS operations.
QoS Profiles — Default QoS applied to all DataWriters/DataReaders unless overridden. Common defaults: RELIABLE, VOLATILE, KEEP_LAST with depth=10.
Network Interface Binding — The interface name (eth0) determines which physical network DDS uses. Wrong interface = cannot reach the robot even on the same switch.
slides.bottom_band: Most common Day 5 failure: CYCLONEDDS_URI not set or wrong interface name. Always verify these before debugging anything else. ChannelFactoryInitialize is your first diagnostic checkpoint.

slides.title: Message Flow in G1 DDS
slides.thesis: A complete DDS message journey: Publisher creates data → DataWriter serializes and sends → Topic routes to matched DataReaders → Subscriber callback fires. Every step has potential failure points — knowing the flow means knowing where to look.

rows: 1. Write
App → DataWriter
Application calls write() with typed data sample.
DataWriter not created or wrong type.
Check DataWriter creation return code.
2. Serialize
DataWriter → Network
DataWriter serializes sample and sends via UDP/shared memory.
Network interface down or firewall blocking.
ping robot IP; check interface status.
3. Match
DDS Discovery
DDS matches DataWriter to DataReader(s) with same Topic + Type + compatible QoS.
QoS incompatibility prevents matching.
Use dds_participant_info to check match status.
4. Deliver
Network → DataReader
DataReader receives and deserializes sample into typed cache.
DataReader cache full (history depth exceeded).
Increase history depth or consume faster.
5. Callback
DataReader → App
DataReader invokes registered callback with received sample.
Callback not registered or throws exception.
Check Init() call with callback function pointer.

slides.bottom_band: Trace this flow for rt/lowstate on Day 5: verify the DataWriter exists (on the robot), confirm your DataReader is matched, and observe callbacks firing with valid data.

slides.title: DDS Discovery Assumptions
slides.thesis: DDS discovery is automatic but relies on specific network conditions: multicast reachability, shared subnet, compatible protocols. When discovery fails, participants are invisible to each other even if the physical network is functional.
board_data: Multicast Reachability — SDP uses multicast (239.255.0.1 default) for participant announcement. Blocked multicast = silent discovery failure. Fix: configure unicast peer list.
Shared Subnet — Participants must be on the same IP subnet for multicast discovery. Operator and G1 must have IPs in same range (typically 192.168.123.x).
Compatible Protocols — Both ends must use same DDS implementation. G1 uses CycloneDDS. Mismatched implementations can have subtle discovery incompatibilities.
No NAT/VPN Interference — NAT and VPN tunnels break multicast and alter packet headers. Always use a direct, un-NATted connection to the robot network.
Domain ID Match — Discovery is scoped to a domain ID. Domain 0 and domain 1 are separate universes. Verify both sides use the same domain ID.
slides.bottom_band: Discovery failure is the #2 most common Day 5 issue. Symptom: subscriber Init() succeeds but callbacks never fire. Diagnosis: check multicast, subnet, and domain ID.

slides.title: Beginner-Friendly Communication Checks
slides.thesis: Before writing custom code, verify DDS health with diagnostic commands. These tools confirm DDS is running, topics exist, and messages flow — the prerequisites for any custom subscriber or publisher.

rows: dds_topic_list
List all active DDS topics on the domain.
rt/lowstate, rt/locomotion, api/* and other G1 topics.
Check DDS domain ID, network interface, CycloneDDS config.
dds_msg_echo <topic>
Print messages published on a topic in real time.
Continuous stream of lowstate messages with tick incrementing.
Topic does not exist or no publisher. Check robot power and DDS init.
dds_participant_info
List all active DDS participants and their entities.
Robot + your workstation = at least 2 participants.
Your workstation did not join domain. Check ChannelFactoryInitialize.
Verify basic IP connectivity to the robot.
Successful ping replies with < 5 ms latency.
Physical network: cable, switch, or robot not powered on.
ip link show
List network interfaces and their status.
eth0 (or robot interface) shows UP state.
Interface down or named differently. Check ifconfig/ip link.

slides.bottom_band: Run these checks in order like a pre-flight checklist. If ping fails, nothing above works. Build the habit: diagnose bottom-up, not top-down.

slides.title: DDS Quality of Service (QoS) Policies
slides.thesis: QoS policies are the contract between publisher and subscriber — they control reliability, durability, history, deadline, and liveliness. A QoS mismatch prevents communication silently. Understanding each policy is essential for building custom classes.

rows: RELIABILITY
RELIABLE vs BEST_EFFORT
RELIABLE guarantees delivery with retries. BEST_EFFORT sends once.
rt/lowstate: BEST_EFFORT (high rate). Safety commands: RELIABLE (must deliver).
DURABILITY
VOLATILE vs TRANSIENT_LOCAL
VOLATILE: only new data. TRANSIENT_LOCAL: late joiners get last N samples.
Status topics: TRANSIENT_LOCAL. Streaming: VOLATILE.
HISTORY
KEEP_LAST(depth) vs KEEP_ALL
KEEP_LAST stores N most recent. KEEP_ALL stores every sample.
Motor commands: KEEP_LAST(1). Sensor logs: KEEP_ALL.
DEADLINE
Period + missed deadline action
Publisher promises data rate. Missed deadline triggers listener = safety event.
Watchdog heartbeat: DEADLINE(100ms). Missed 200ms → trigger fallback.
LIVELINESS
AUTOMATIC vs MANUAL_BY_TOPIC
How DDS detects if participant is alive. MANUAL requires explicit assertion.
Safety monitoring: MANUAL_BY_TOPIC with short lease. Lease expires → dead.

slides.bottom_band: QoS is not configuration boilerplate — it is the safety contract. A DEADLINE policy on a watchdog IS a safety mechanism. Choose QoS deliberately, not from defaults.

slides.title: Data Writers and Data Readers
slides.thesis: While we speak of Publishers and Subscribers conceptually, the actual DDS entities that send and receive data are DataWriters and DataReaders. Each is bound to a specific Topic and DataType — type-specific interfaces ensure data integrity.

board_data.label: Publisher → DataWriter
board_data.value: A Publisher is a container for DataWriters. Each DataWriter is created with a specific Topic (name + type). The Publisher manages lifecycle and QoS grouping. One Publisher can hold multiple DataWriters.

board_data.label: Subscriber → DataReader
board_data.value: A Subscriber contains DataReaders. Each DataReader registers a callback for a specific Topic. When matched DataWriters publish, the DataReader deserializes and invokes the callback on a DDS thread.

board_data.label: Type-Specific Binding
board_data.value: DataWriter<LowState> can only write LowState samples. DataReader<LowState> can only read LowState samples. Types are generated from IDL at compile time — Python bindings use unitree_sdk2py generated types.

board_data.label: QoS Per Reader/Writer
board_data.value: QoS is negotiated per DataWriter/DataReader pair, not per Topic. Two DataReaders on the same Topic can have different QoS — enabling different subscribers to receive the same data with different guarantees.

slides.title: API Registration and Custom Classes — Introduction
slides.thesis: API registration is how your code becomes a recognized G1 service. Custom Publisher and Subscriber classes are how you send and receive typed data with explicit QoS. Together, they form the programmatic interface to G1.
board_data: API (Application Programming Interface) — A defined set of functions, inputs, and outputs that your code exposes to or consumes from the G1 DDS ecosystem.
Custom Publisher Class — Inherits from DDS publisher base. Binds to Topic + DataType. Exposes publish() with pre-send validation. Configures QoS explicitly at creation.
Custom Subscriber Class — Inherits from DDS subscriber base. Binds to Topic + DataType. Registers on_data_received() callback. Must match publisher QoS to receive data.
Registration Pipeline — 1) Define data type (IDL or Python struct). 2) Register API with unique ID. 3) Create custom publisher/subscriber with explicit QoS. 4) Initialize and verify with dry run.
slides.bottom_band: Custom classes are where DDS theory becomes operational code. Build your classes to the same standard as the SDK own — a well-built custom publisher is indistinguishable from a built-in one.

slides.title: Registering APIs
slides.thesis: API registration assigns your code a unique identity in the G1 service ecosystem. The process: define interface → assign unique ID → register with service discovery → verify discoverability. An unregistered API is invisible to other components.

rows: 1. Define Interface
Specify API name, version, input parameters, return types, error codes.
Document the API contract completely. Peer review before registration.
API definition document signed off by instructor.
2. Assign Unique ID
Reserve an API ID (e.g., 7001–7099 for custom services).
Ensure no collision with existing SDK-reserved ID ranges.
List registered APIs; confirm ID uniqueness.
3. Register Service
Call registration function with API definition and ID.
Handle registration failure: duplicate ID, invalid definition.
Registration returns success code. API appears in service listings.
4. Verify Discovery
Other participants can discover and describe your API.
Test from a different DDS participant: can it find and describe your API?
Service discovery tool lists your API with correct metadata.

slides.bottom_band: API registration is your code handshake with G1. No registration = invisible to safety monitors, undiscoverable by other services. Always register before testing; always verify registration succeeded.

slides.title: Building Custom Publisher Classes
slides.thesis: A custom publisher class encapsulates everything needed to send typed data on a G1 DDS topic: topic selection, type binding, QoS configuration, and publish() with validation. Build once, verify thoroughly, reuse across labs.
board_data: Step 1 — Inherit from Base Publisher: Use SDK publisher base class. Handles participant lifecycle, topic creation, and DataWriter management.
Step 2 — Define Data Type: Import generated Python type from unitree_sdk2py (e.g., from unitree_sdk2py.idl.unitree_hg.msg import LowState_). Must match topic IDL exactly.
Step 3 — Create DataWriter with Explicit QoS: Set RELIABILITY, DURABILITY, HISTORY depth, and DEADLINE period. Do not rely on defaults — declare explicitly.
Step 4 — Implement publish(data): Write method accepting typed data, calling writer.write(data). Add pre-publish validation: check fields within valid ranges before sending.
Step 5 — Dry-Run Verification: Before live publish, create a test subscriber. Verify: correct topic, all fields match, no QoS mismatch warnings. The 5-minute verification saves hours of debugging.
slides.bottom_band: A custom publisher that skips Step 5 is a safety hazard. Always dry-run verify with a subscriber before connecting to a live robot.

slides.title: Building Custom Subscriber Classes
slides.thesis: A custom subscriber class encapsulates everything needed to receive and process typed data from a G1 DDS topic: topic binding, type matching, QoS compatibility, and a callback that fires on every new sample.
board_data: Step 1 — Inherit from Base Subscriber: SDK subscriber base class. Handles participant lifecycle, topic subscription, and DataReader management.
Step 2 — Match Publisher Data Type: Import the same generated type the publisher uses. Type mismatch = no communication. Verify against topic IDL.

Step 3 — Create DataReader with Compatible QoS: QoS must be compatible (not identical). RELIABLE writer + BEST_EFFORT reader = downgraded delivery. TRANSIENT_LOCAL vs VOLATILE incompatibility blocks matching.

Step 4 — Implement on_data_received(msg): Callback for each incoming sample. Keep callbacks fast — DDS delivers on its own thread. Offload heavy processing to separate thread/queue. Never block the DDS callback thread.
Step 5 — Verify with Known Source: Subscribe to built-in topic (rt/lowstate) first to confirm subscriber infrastructure works before connecting to custom publisher.
slides.bottom_band: Subscriber callbacks are the entry point for all robot data. Write defensive callbacks: catch exceptions, log errors, never block. A silent exception in a callback creates a blind spot.

slides.title: Organizing Message Handlers
slides.thesis: As custom classes grow, message handlers must stay organized: modular functions per message type, event-driven dispatch, and robust error handling. Disorganized handlers produce spaghetti code — and spaghetti code misses safety events.

board_data.label: Modular Functions
board_data.value: Break processing into single-responsibility functions: parse_lowstate(), check_imu_orientation(), classify_fsm_mode(). Each function does one thing. Test independently.

board_data.label: Event-Driven Architecture
board_data.value: Dispatch on message content: if mode_machine changed → fire state_transition_event. If tick stalls → fire communication_stall_event. Higher-level handlers subscribe to these events — decoupled and testable.

board_data.label: Error Handling
board_data.value: Every handler must handle: malformed data, missing fields, out-of-range values, and callback exceptions. Catch, log, continue — never crash the subscriber.

board_data.label: Structured Logging
board_data.value: DEBUG for every sample (development). INFO for state transitions. WARNING for anomalous values. ERROR for callback failures. JSON lines format enables automated analysis.

slides.bottom_band: Handler organization is a safety property. When a watchdog heartbeat stops, your handler has milliseconds to detect it. If buried in spaghetti, it will miss the event.

slides.title: Validating Command/Feedback Loops
slides.thesis: Every command must be validated by observing its feedback — send command, receive feedback, verify response. This loop proves your code affects the robot as intended. On Day 5, practice this with read-only API calls before Day 6 motion commands.

rows: Pre-Command
Read lowstate before any command.
Current tick, mode_machine, motor_state, IMU all populated and valid.
All fields within expected ranges. Robot in known stable state.
Command Send
Issue via custom publisher or API call.
Return code = 0 (success).
Non-zero → command rejected. Do not proceed.
Immediate Feedback
Read lowstate < 100 ms after command.
Tick incremented. mode_machine may show transitional state.
Tick must be monotonic. If tick stalls, DDS is down. Abort.
Steady-State Verify
Monitor lowstate for N seconds post-stabilization.
mode_machine returns to stable. motor_state matches expected values.
If transitional > timeout, robot is stuck. Trigger recovery.

slides.bottom_band: Command/feedback validation is not optional — it is the only way to know your command was received, accepted, and correctly executed. Skipping it is operating blind.

slides.title: API Versioning and Compatibility
slides.thesis: G1 APIs evolve over SDK versions. Semantic versioning (Major.Minor.Patch), backward compatibility guarantees, and deprecation strategies ensure your custom classes survive SDK updates — and fail loudly when incompatible.
board_data: Semantic Versioning — MAJOR.MINOR.patch: Major = breaking changes. Minor = new features, backward compatible. Patch = bug fixes only.
Backward Compatibility — Code written for SDK v2.x.y works on SDK v2.x.z (same major.minor). NOT guaranteed across major versions.
Deprecation Strategy — APIs marked @deprecated for one minor version before removal. Migrate during the deprecation window when warnings appear.
Version Check at Startup — Custom classes should check SDK_VERSION. Mismatched major version → refuse to initialize, log clear error. Never silently behave differently.
slides.bottom_band: Version mismatches are a real safety risk. An API that swapped parameter order between versions executes with swapped values. Check versions at startup.

slides.title: Error Handling Patterns in Custom Classes
slides.thesis: Robust custom classes handle errors at every layer: initialization failures, DDS communication errors, data validation failures, and callback exceptions. Unhandled errors crash subscribers — taking observability offline.

rows: Initialization Failure
Fail Fast
If ChannelFactoryInitialize or DataReader creation fails, raise immediately. Do not proceed degraded.
Fix configuration and restart.
Transient DDS Error
Retry with Backoff
write() failure → retry 3× with exponential backoff (100ms, 200ms, 400ms).
Most transient errors resolve within 1s.
Invalid Incoming Data
Validate and Skip
Check required fields present, values in range. Skip invalid samples; log warning.
One bad sample should not crash subscriber.
Callback Exception
Catch, Log, Continue
Wrap every callback in try/except. Log with traceback. Never let exception escape.
Subscriber continues; bad sample logged.
Persistent Failure
Graceful Degradation
No data > N seconds → degraded mode: log ERROR, notify operator, attempt reconnect.
Preserves state; alerts operator.

slides.bottom_band: Error handling separates production code from prototype code. On a safety-critical system, silent failure is the worst failure mode — a subscriber that stops without logging is a ticking time bomb.

slides.title: Structural Safety and Fail-Safe Control — Introduction
slides.thesis: Safety on G1 is a layered architecture of independent mechanisms: structural safety prevents physical damage, fail-safe control ensures predictable behavior during faults, emergency termination provides hardware-level override, and redundancy provides backup for critical functions.

board_data.label: Layer 1: Structural Safety
board_data.value: Software-enforced physical limits: joint angle boundaries, torque ceilings, collision detection, force thresholds. Prevents damage during normal operation. Runs at 100 Hz–1 kHz.

board_data.label: Layer 2: Fail-Safe Control
board_data.value: Predictable behavior when things go wrong: watchdogs detect dead processes, state machines define safe transitions, controlled stops bring robot to rest gracefully.

board_data.label: Layer 3: Emergency Termination
board_data.value: Hardware-level override: motor power cut-off via physical relay, brake engagement. Bypasses all software. Takes < 50 ms from trigger to zero motor torque.

board_data.label: Layer 4: Redundancy
board_data.value: Multiple independent paths: dual sensors cross-check, redundant actuators provide backup, voting mechanisms resolve conflicts. Always active — not triggered, always on.

slides.bottom_band: No single failure produces an unsafe state. If structural safety is exceeded, fail-safe triggers. If fail-safe does not respond, emergency termination cuts power. Each layer is independent — no shared fate.

slides.title: Implementing Structural Safety Loops
slides.thesis: Structural safety loops continuously monitor joint limits, collision indicators, and force/torque thresholds. When a limit is exceeded, the loop triggers a safety response — controlled stop or immediate damp. These loops are your first line of defense.

rows: Joint Limit Monitor
Encoder position vs. mechanical limits per DOF.
Soft: 95% of hard. Hard: physical end.
Soft limit → reduce speed. Hard limit → immediate damp + log.
Collision Detection
Motor current spikes + unexpected IMU acceleration.
Current > 150% expected. IMU > 3σ from prediction.
Immediate damp + log with timestamp and joint snapshot.
Force/Torque Threshold
Ankle F/T sensors: ground reaction forces.
ZMP near polygon edge. Vertical force < 20% body weight.
Reduce velocity. ZMP exits polygon → emergency stop.
Communication Stall
Tick field in rt/lowstate — must increment.
Tick unchanged > 200 ms (2 missed cycles at 10 Hz).
Communication lost alarm. Damp after 500 ms silence.

slides.bottom_band: A joint limit monitor that works prevents a motor from destroying its own gearbox. A collision detector that works prevents the robot from hurting someone. These are not optional features.

slides.title: Real-Time Fallback Watchdogs
slides.thesis: A watchdog monitors system health via periodic heartbeats. Missed heartbeat → component declared dead → fallback action triggered (damp + alarm). Watchdogs are the last software defense before hardware emergency stops.
board_data: Periodic Heartbeat — Each critical component sends heartbeat at fixed interval (e.g., 100 ms): component ID + timestamp + status code. Simple, lightweight, reliable.
Timeout Detection — No heartbeat within timeout (2–3× interval) → component declared dead. Default: 100ms interval × 3 = 300ms detection time.
Fallback Action — On timeout: (1) Log failure with component ID and last status. (2) Command damp mode. (3) Notify operator with alarm. (4) Disable further commands until reset.
Meta-Watchdog — Hardware timer monitors the software watchdog process. If software watchdog crashes, hardware timer triggers emergency termination directly. Who watches the watchmen? Hardware does.
Recovery — After watchdog damp: (1) Confirm robot stable. (2) Investigate logs. (3) Fix root cause. (4) Reset watchdog. (5) Re-init communication. (6) Instructor approval to resume.
slides.bottom_band: The watchdog is your silent guardian. When it triggers, something already failed — the watchdog ensures that failure does not become a catastrophe.

slides.title: Controlled Stop Behavior
slides.thesis: A controlled stop is graceful deceleration that maintains balance while bringing the robot to rest. The deceleration profile, balance maintenance, and safe posture transition minimize stress and prevent secondary hazards.

board_data.label: Deceleration Profile
board_data.value: Smooth velocity ramp (trapezoidal or S-curve) from current velocity to zero. Duration: 0.5–2.0s. Jerk limiting prevents sudden acceleration changes that could destabilize balance.

board_data.label: Balance Maintenance
board_data.value: Balance controller stays active during stop. ZMP continuously monitored within support polygon. If ZMP approaches edge, stop profile adjusts to prioritize balance over stopping time.

board_data.label: Safe Posture
board_data.value: After zero velocity: knees slightly bent (lower CoM), arms at sides, feet shoulder-width. Maximizes support polygon, minimizes potential energy — most stable configuration.

board_data.label: Post-Stop Verification
board_data.value: Verify: mode_machine shows damp/stand (stable), IMU near-level, motor_state shows zero velocity, ZMP centered. Log all readings before any next action.

slides.bottom_band: A controlled stop is your primary safety tool. Practice until it is muscle memory: STOP → VERIFY → DECIDE.

slides.title: Emergency Termination Logic
slides.thesis: Emergency termination is the last-resort safety measure: immediate motor power cut-off via physical relay, brake engagement, and hardware-level override. It bypasses all software — no code can prevent it, no bug can delay it.
board_data: Immediate Power Cut-off — Motor drivers disconnected via physical relay. NOT a software command — electrical disconnect. Motors go to zero torque instantly.
Brake Engagement — Passive electromagnetic brakes engage on power loss (fail-safe: brakes require power to release). Locks joints, prevents collapse under weight.
Hardware-Level Override — E-stop circuit is independent of main computer, DDS, and all software. Physical button → physical relay. Pressing it breaks a circuit — sends no message.
Triggers — Hardware E-stop button (operator panel), wireless E-stop (instructor remote), software-triggered (last resort from safety controller), automatic on power failure.
Post-E-Stop Recovery — (1) Secure scene. (2) Investigate root cause. (3) Document event. (4) Physical inspection. (5) Instructor sign-off. (6) Power-on in damp. (7) Full readiness check.
slides.bottom_band: The E-stop is not a debugging tool — it is a safety device. Never use E-stop for convenience. False-alarm E-stops desensitize the team to real emergencies.

slides.title: Software Termination Scripts
slides.thesis: Software termination scripts provide orderly shutdown: close DDS participants, release resources, log events, verify safe state. A clean shutdown is as important as a clean startup — ghost participants and leaked resources degrade the system.

rows: Pre-Check
Verify robot in damp or stand. Confirm no active velocity commands.
Never shut down while robot is moving. Script must refuse if not in safe state.
DDS Teardown
Delete in reverse creation order: DataWriters → Publishers → DataReaders → Subscribers → DomainParticipant.
Reverse order avoids dangling references and resource leaks.
Resource Release
Close file handles, free memory, stop threads, join thread pools.
Resource leaks accumulate across sessions and degrade performance.
Event Logging
Timestamped shutdown event: reason, final state snapshot, resource release confirmations.
Last known state for post-mortem analysis if something went wrong.
User Notification
Print: All DDS closed. Resources released. Robot in damp. Safe to power off.
Operator must know definitively that shutdown completed — no ambiguity.

slides.bottom_band: A termination script that skips DDS teardown leaves ghost participants on the network. Always clean up. Always verify cleanup succeeded.

slides.title: Redundancy in Sensors and Actuators
slides.thesis: Redundancy means critical functions have backup: multiple sensors cross-check, redundant actuators take over, voting mechanisms resolve conflicts. Redundancy converts catastrophic failures into manageable degraded-mode events.

board_data.label: Sensor Redundancy
board_data.value: Critical measurements (joint position, IMU) have ≥2 independent sensors. Physically impossible reading → trust backup, flag faulty sensor for maintenance.

board_data.label: Actuator Redundancy
board_data.value: Redundant motors per joint: one fails → other maintains position at reduced torque. Controller detects via current mismatch, reconfigures dynamically.

board_data.label: Voting Mechanisms
board_data.value: 3+ redundant sensors: majority vote (2 of 3) or median. Consistently disagreeing sensor → quarantined — data ignored until re-validated.

board_data.label: Graceful Degradation
board_data.value: Redundancy exhausted → reduced capability mode: slower speeds, wider safety margins. Continues operating — but more conservatively.

slides.bottom_band: Single sensor failure without redundancy = emergency stop. Single sensor failure with redundancy = log, switch to backup, continue with caution. That is the value of redundancy.

slides.title: Fail-Safe Control — State Machine Design
slides.thesis: Fail-safe behavior is designed as a state machine: each state defines permissible actions, every transition has entry/exit conditions, and safety-critical transitions (to FALLBACK or EMERGENCY_STOP) can trigger from any state.

rows: NORMAL
Full operation: locomotion, arms, audio, sensors.
Watchdog timeout, joint violation, collision, E-stop.
WARNING (minor) or FALLBACK (major) or E-STOP (critical).
WARNING
Reduced: slow speeds, no new commands, intensified monitoring.
Anomaly resolved → return. Escalated → FALLBACK. Timeout 5s → FALLBACK.
NORMAL (resolved) or FALLBACK (escalated).
FALLBACK
Controlled stop in progress. No new commands. Damp target.
Stop complete → SAFE. Stop failed → E-STOP.
SAFE (complete) or E-STOP (escalated).
SAFE
Robot damp, zero torque, brakes engaged. Read-only.
Operator reset + instructor approval.
NORMAL (reset).
E-STOP
All motors power-cut, brakes engaged, hardware override.
None — terminal. Requires physical inspection and manual reset.
SAFE (after inspection + power cycle).

slides.bottom_band: The state machine is your safety contract. Every state has defined behavior. Every transition has defined conditions. No surprises — the state machine determines the response, not an operator split-second decision.

slides.title: Software Termination — Resource Management
slides.thesis: Proper resource management during termination prevents orphaned DDS participants, unclosed files, unfreed memory, and zombie threads — all of which degrade the system and interfere with subsequent sessions.
board_data: DDS Participant Shutdown — Delete in strict reverse order: DataWriters → Publisher → DataReaders → Subscriber → DomainParticipant. Verify each deletion returns success.
File Handle Release — Close all log files, configs, data recordings. Use context managers (with statement) for guaranteed cleanup. Verify zero open handles after termination.
Memory Deallocation — Release buffers, message objects, data structures. Explicitly delete circular references that prevent garbage collection.
Thread/Process Termination — Signal all background threads to stop. Join with timeout. Hung thread → log and force-exit — a hung thread should not prevent shutdown.
Verification — Post-termination diagnostics: list DDS participants (should be zero), check open file handles (should be none), check memory (should return to baseline).
slides.bottom_band: A single leaked DDS participant can cause discovery storms that degrade the entire robot network. Clean up what you create. Verify you cleaned it up.

slides.title: Lab Validation — Proving It Works
slides.thesis: Lab validation systematically verifies every component and interaction in a controlled environment before the robot is trusted operationally. Validation produces evidence — not feelings of confidence.
board_data: Controlled Environment — Test with known conditions: flat floor, no obstacles, instructor present, E-stop tested. Eliminate environmental variables so failures come only from code or robot.
Systematic Testing — Test each component in isolation, then integrated, then end-to-end. A passing unit test that fails integration tells you exactly where the interface breaks.
Data Collection — Every test produces data: DDS topic logs, API call traces, lowstate snapshots, safety trigger events. Data proves what happened. Without data, you have opinions.
Reproducibility — Every test must be reproducible: same conditions, same commands, same outcomes. Passes once + fails once = race condition or uninitialized state — not a passing test.
slides.bottom_band: Validation is not I ran it and it did not crash. Validation is I ran these specific tests, collected this specific evidence, and all results matched expected outcomes. The difference is auditable proof.

slides.title: Validation Evidence Checklist
slides.thesis: Every lab session must produce a documented evidence trail. This evidence is your proof of safe operation — and your diagnostic data when something goes wrong. If it is not logged, it did not happen.

rows: DDS Topic Logs
rt/lowstate: tick, mode_machine, IMU, motor_state for full session.
Timestamped JSON lines or binary log.
Keep entire course — compare across days.
API Call Traces
Every API call: timestamp, API ID, parameters, return code, latency.
[ts] API_ID={id} params={...} rc={code} latency_ms={ms}
Keep all sessions for readiness classification.
Safety Trigger Reports
Every safety event: watchdog timeout, limit violation, E-stop, controlled stop.
[ts] EVENT={type} before={state} after={state} reason={...}
Keep permanently for incident review.
System State Snapshots
Full lowstate at: session start, after each lab, session end.
Complete rt/lowstate dump with all fields.
Cross-session comparison and trend analysis.

slides.bottom_band: Build the evidence habit on Day 5 when you are only observing. By Day 7, evidence collection should be automatic.

slides.title: Readiness Review — The Final Gate
slides.thesis: The readiness review is the final gate before any operational task: documentation review, team briefing, scenario walkthrough, and safety sign-off. This is the last chance to catch errors before they become incidents.
board_data: Documentation Review — All procedures documented. All APIs registered. All QoS reviewed. All safety scripts tested. Documentation gaps = knowledge gaps.
Team Briefing — Every operator knows: today objectives, their role, stop authority, abort conditions, emergency procedures. No surprises during the session.
Scenario Walkthroughs — Rehearse: What if robot loses balance? What if DDS drops? What if someone enters exclusion zone? Team has rehearsed responses to every scenario.
Final Safety Sign-off — Instructor/safety officer confirms: readiness checklist complete, team briefed, scenarios rehearsed, E-stop tested, exclusion zone clear. No sign-off = no operation.
slides.bottom_band: A thorough readiness review catches configuration errors and unaligned expectations. A rushed review lets them all through. Take the time.

slides.title: Debugging Habits for G1 Communication
slides.thesis: Systematic debugging saves hours: start simple, isolate components, check QoS, use DDS tools. The most common bugs are the simplest — check them first. Diagnose bottom-up, not top-down.

rows: 1. Physical
Ethernet connected? Interface UP?
Cable unplugged, wrong port.
Check connection. Run ip link show.
2. IP
Can you ping 192.168.123.19?
Wrong IP, firewall, subnet mismatch.
Verify robot IP and your subnet.
3. DDS Env
CYCLONEDDS_URI set? Config valid?
Env var not set, XML syntax error.
Export CYCLONEDDS_URI. Validate XML.
4. Participant
ChannelFactoryInitialize return success?
Wrong domain ID, interface binding failed.
Check domain ID (default 0). Verify interface.
5. Discovery
Subscriber sees the topic?
Topic name typo, type mismatch, QoS incompatible.
Use dds_topic_list. Verify exact name.
6. Data Flow
Callback firing? Data valid?
Callback not registered, deserialization fails.
Add debug print. Verify type matches IDL.

slides.bottom_band: If you start debugging callback logic and the Ethernet cable is unplugged, you have wasted an hour. Always verify the physical layer first, then work up the stack.

slides.title: Evidence Habits for G1 Safety
slides.thesis: Cultivate evidence habits: log everything, timestamp events, automate collection, review regularly. Evidence is for continuous improvement — today logs prevent tomorrow incidents. Treat near-misses as invaluable data.
board_data: Log Everything — Every DDS message, API call, state transition, safety event. Storage is cheap. Missing data is expensive. Filter later; you cannot recover uncaptured data.
Timestamp Everything — ISO 8601 with milliseconds. Correlating distributed events requires synchronized clocks. A log without timestamps is a story without a timeline.
Automate Collection — Scripts capture lowstate at session boundaries. Subscribers log all messages. API wrappers record every call. If a human must remember to collect it, it will be forgotten.
Review Regularly — After every session: look for anomalous values, unexpected transitions, communication gaps, and trends. Patterns across sessions are invisible in a single log.
Near-Misses Are Data — A safety trigger that fired without damage reveals a vulnerability without the cost of an incident. Investigate every near-miss as thoroughly as an actual incident.
slides.bottom_band: Professional operators have evidence of exactly what happened, when, and why. Amateurs have opinions. Build evidence habits from Day 5.

slides.title: Validation Gates for G1 Deployment
slides.thesis: Before G1 can be trusted operationally, it passes sequential validation gates: functional testing, safety protocol verification, performance benchmarks, and human-robot interaction review. Each gate must be passed — in order.

rows: 1. Functional
All core functions: DDS, lowstate, API registration, FSM queries.
DDS logs with continuous lowstate. API traces with success codes.
Any function fails or returns unexpected errors.
2. Safety Protocol
All safety mechanisms: watchdog triggers, controlled stop, E-stop.
Safety trigger reports with timestamps within spec.
Any mechanism fails or response time exceeds spec.
3. Performance
Message latency < 1ms, tick rate ≥ 100Hz, motor temps within limits.
Benchmark reports with measured vs expected values.
Any metric out of range for > 5 seconds.
4. HRI Review
Operator understands indicators, alarms distinguishable, controls intuitive.
HRI checklist completed by independent reviewer.
Any interaction rated confusing or unsafe.

slides.bottom_band: Gates are sequential: no point testing safety if basic functions fail. No point measuring performance if safety is not confirmed. Respect the gate order.

slides.title: G1 Safety Loops — Recap
slides.thesis: G1 safety is four independent loops forming defense-in-depth: structural safety (prevents damage), fallback watchdogs (detect dead processes), controlled stop (graceful deceleration), and emergency termination (hardware cut-off).

board_data.label: Loop 1: Structural Safety
board_data.value: Joint limits, collision detection, force thresholds. Violation → controlled stop or damp. Runs in motor controller firmware at 100 Hz–1 kHz.

board_data.label: Loop 2: Fallback Watchdogs
board_data.value: Heartbeat monitoring of all software components. Missed heartbeat → component dead → damp. Separate process with its own hardware watchdog.

board_data.label: Loop 3: Controlled Stop
board_data.value: Smooth deceleration, balance maintenance, safe posture. Triggered by safety violations or operator command. 0.5–2.0s to complete.

board_data.label: Loop 4: Emergency Termination
board_data.value: Hardware power cut-off, brake engagement. Bypasses all software. Triggered by E-stop button or critical fault. < 50ms to zero torque.

slides.bottom_band: Defense-in-depth: an adversary (or bug) that defeats one loop is stopped by the next. The only way all four fail is if they share a common failure — which is why they are independent.

slides.title: Watchdog and Fallback Logic — Key Takeaways
slides.thesis: Watchdogs embody three principles: proactive monitoring (continuous checks, not reactive), timely response (milliseconds matter), and predictable behavior (always to a known safe state, never improvises).
board_data: Proactive Monitoring — Watchdogs do not wait for operators to notice problems. They continuously check at fixed intervals. If the operator notices before the watchdog, the interval is too long.
Timely Response — Detection (300ms with 3× heartbeat timeout) + fallback execution (2s controlled stop) = < 2.3s from failure to safe state. Every millisecond counts.
Predictable Behavior — Fallback action is defined before deployment, not chosen at failure time. Every operator knows: if watchdog fires, robot will do X. No improvisation.
Watchdog on the Watchdog — Hardware timer monitors the software watchdog process. Software watchdog crashes → hardware timer triggers emergency termination. Hardware is the final authority.

slides.title: Software Termination Scripts — Best Practices
slides.thesis: Well-written termination scripts follow four principles: idempotency (running twice is safe), thorough resource cleanup (nothing left behind), user notification (operator always knows status), and rigorous testing (verified in all scenarios).
board_data: Idempotency — Running twice = same result as once. Already-deleted DDS entities → detect and skip, do not crash. Every cleanup action checks state before attempting.
Thorough Cleanup — Checklist: DDS participants deleted, files closed, memory freed, threads joined, temp files removed, logs flushed. Verify each item. Report failures.
User Notification — Clear status per phase: [OK] DDS deleted. [OK] Files closed (3). [WARN] Thread sensor_poll hung — forced exit. Operator never wonders what is happening.
Test All Scenarios — Test: normal shutdown, post-DDS-error, hung thread, double-run (idempotency), from every FSM state. If it fails in any scenario, fix it.
slides.bottom_band: A termination script is production code, not a throwaway utility. A bug in a publisher causes a communication error. A bug in termination leaves the robot in an undefined state.

slides.title: Day 5 Wrap-Up — Mission Accomplished
slides.thesis: Day 5 established the foundational competencies for G1 humanoid operation: platform understanding, DDS communication architecture, custom class development, and layered safety mechanisms. Every subsequent day builds on this foundation.

rows: G1 Platform Overview
Subsystems, bipedal mechanics, hardware variants, actuator/sensor roles, balance assumptions, safe boundaries.
Complete
G1 DDS Communications
Peer-to-peer topology, topic structures, message flow, initialization, discovery, communication verification.
Complete
API Registration & Custom Classes
API registration, custom publisher/subscriber with QoS, message handlers, command/feedback validation.
Complete
Safety Loops & Watchdogs
Layered safety: structural loops, watchdogs, controlled stop, emergency termination, termination scripts.
Complete
Validation & Professional Habits
Evidence collection, readiness gates, debugging methodology, evidence discipline.
Complete

slides.bottom_band: Day 5 transforms students from robot observers to safety-conscious operators who understand communication, build custom interfaces, and trust nothing without evidence. Ready for Day 6: gated motion and arm control.

slides.title: Next Steps and Resources
slides.thesis: Your G1 journey continues with Day 6 (gated locomotion and arm control), hands-on labs, comprehensive documentation, and the developer community. Apply today no-motion discipline to tomorrow motion commands.

board_data.label: Practice Exercises
board_data.value: Apply Day 5 concepts in three labs: G1 readiness gate, subscribe to rt/lowstate, and read-only FSM inspection. Complete all with full evidence collection before Day 6.

board_data.label: G1 Documentation

board_data.label: Community Forums
board_data.value: Engage with G1 developers: share debugging stories, ask QoS questions, learn from others mistakes. The community has solved problems you will encounter.

board_data.label: Day 6 Preview
board_data.value: Gated locomotion via LocoClient, arm actions via G1ArmActionClient, integration policy for non-conflicting control. The FSM you observed today will be the FSM you command tomorrow.

slides.bottom_band: Day 5 was understanding and observing. Day 6 is commanding — always gated, always reversible, always with confirmed stops. Today discipline makes tomorrow motion safe.

slides.title: References & Further Reading
slides.thesis: Recommended resources for deepening understanding of DDS, humanoid robotics, and safety-critical systems.
board_data: CycloneDDS Documentation — https://cyclonedds.io/docs/ — Configuration, QoS, transport, and troubleshooting for the DDS implementation used by G1.
OMG DDS Specification — https://www.omg.org/spec/DDS/ — Formal DDS standard. Deep reading for QoS, discovery, and type system details.
ROS 2 DDS Context — https://docs.ros.org/en/humble/Concepts/About-Quality-of-Service-Settings.html — Practical QoS guidance applicable to any DDS system.
Unitree SDK Documentation — Official unitree_sdk2py API reference, IDL definitions, and G1 hardware specifications. Primary technical reference.
Humanoid Robotics Reference — Textbook and research literature on bipedal locomotion, ZMP control, and humanoid stability for deeper theoretical understanding.
