labs.title: G1 Architecture, Environment & Readiness Gate
labs.content:
Duration:~45–60 min (theory + checks)
Robot required:Optional for Sections A–C; required for Section D
Motion:None in this lab
🎯Learning Objectives
  • Describe how your PC talks to G1 over Ethernet and DDS (not ROS topics).
  • Explain why G1 code uses unitree_hg IDL types and the sport locomotion service.
  • Install and verify unitree_sdk2_python + CycloneDDS on your machine.
  • Run the readiness checks and interpret PASS / PARTIAL / FAIL.
  • State the safety rules before any lab that sends motion commands.

1. G1 System Architecture (Conceptual)

The G1 is a humanoid with onboard computers on a fixed subnet. Your laptop joins that LAN over wired Ethernet.

` ┌─────────────────┐ Ethernet ┌──────────────────────────────┐ │ Your PC │ 192.168.123.x │ G1 onboard computers │ │ Python SDK │ ◄──────────────► │ e.g. locomotion @ .161 │ │ CycloneDDS │ UDP multicast │ DDS topics + RPC services │ └─────────────────┘ └──────────────────────────────┘ `

RoleTypical addressNotes
Your PCe.g. 192.168.123.51/24Static IP; same subnet as robot
Locomotion computer192.168.123.161Ping before trusting DDS
Dev computer (optional)192.168.123.164May not respond on all configs
Communication model (SDK2):
PatternUse on G1Example
Topic subscribeHigh-rate robot statert/lowstate → LowState_ (unitree_hg)
Topic publishLow-level commands (Day 6+ / advanced)rt/lowcmd
RPC clientHigh-level sport / loco commandsLocoClient → service "sport"

The Python SDK mirrors the C++ SDK2: same service names, same JSON parameter payloads for RPC.

—-

2. Software Stack

` Your lab scripts │ ▼ unitree_sdk2py ← pip install -e vendor/unitree_sdk2_python │ ├── core.channel ChannelFactoryInitialize, pub/sub ├── g1.loco LocoClient (high-level locomotion) ├── g1.arm (Day 6) └── idl.unitree_hg G1 message types (LowState_, …) │ ▼ CycloneDDS 0.10.x ← CYCLONEDDS_HOME must point to install prefix `

Session Environment (every robot day)

conda activate unitree_env
export CYCLONEDDS_HOME="$HOME/cyclonedds/install"
unset CYCLONEDDS_URI
cd /path/to/repo

Optional: export G1_INTERFACE=en6 (macOS) or enp0s31f6 (Linux) so lab scripts pick your NIC automatically.

—-

3. High-Level Locomotion API (Preview)

Day 5 focuses on LocoClient — RPC to the "sport" service.

CategoryMethods (examples)RPC IDs
Read stateGetFsmId, GetBalanceMode, GetStandHeight7001–7005
Set stateSetFsmId, SetVelocity, SetStandHeight7101–7105
Gestures / tasksSetTaskId → WaveHand, ShakeHand7106
ConvenienceDamp(), Start(), Move(vx,vy,vyaw), StopMove()wraps SetFsmId / SetVelocity
Important FSM shortcuts:
CallFSM idMeaning
Damp()1Damp — robot will not do high-level wave/walk
Start()500Start locomotion stack
Squat2StandUp()706Squat → stand
ZeroTorque()0Zero torque

You will use these in Labs 2–3; Lab 0 only needs the vocabulary.

—-

4. Safety and Operating Rules

Read before any lab that moves the robot.

1. Clear space — 2 m around the robot; no loose cables; floor flat and dry.
2. Feet on the ground — do not hang the robot for high-level tests.
3. Power-on wait — allow ~1 minute after boot before RPC.
4. Do not call Damp() casually — FSM = 1 blocks wave/walk until recovered (stand + remote or SetFsmId(500)).
5. High-level vs low-level — this course uses high-level LocoClient first. Low-level motor commands can conflict with sport mode.
6. Emergency — know how to power off or use the remote estop; keep a spotter for first motion tests.

—-

5. Hands-On

Step 0 — Machine setup (no robot):

conda activate unitree_env
export CYCLONEDDS_HOME="$HOME/cyclonedds/install"
python -c "import unitree_sdk2py; print('OK')"

Step 1 — Network preparation (robot powered, PC wired):

Replace en6 with your interface (enp0s31f6 on Linux, en6 on macOS — find yours with ip -br addr or ifconfig | grep 192.168.123).

• Static IP on robot subnet, e.g. 192.168.123.51/24
• ping -c 2 192.168.123.161 → replies
• unset CYCLONEDDS_URI

Step 2 — Readiness check (robot on, sport mode):

python day-05/lab-00/g1_connection_check.py --interface en6

Example with optional wave smoke test (clear space; FSM must not be 1):

python day-05/lab-00/g1_connection_check.py --interface en6 --try-wave
ResultMeaningNext step
PASSSDK, IP, ping, lowstate, mode ai, FSM ≠ 1Proceed to Lab 1
PARTIAL (exit 2)Network + DDS OK but FSM=1 (damp) or wrong modeStand robot; re-run check
FAIL (exit 1)SDK, IP, ping, or no lowstateFix per step hints; do not run motion labs

The script checks (in order): SDK env → PC IP → ping → optional multicast → DDS rt/lowstate → CheckMode(ai) → FSM id.

—-

6. Exercises

Exercise A — Interface discovery: Record your PC interface name and IP on 192.168.123.0/24. Why is the interface name not 192.168.123.161?

Exercise B — PASS vs PARTIAL: Run g1_connection_check.py and note the summary line. If PARTIAL, what FSM id did you see and what recovery would you try?

Exercise C — IDL package (written): Which IDL package does G1 use — unitree_go or unitree_hg? What happens if you subscribe to rt/lowstate with the wrong type?

Answer

G1 uses unitree_hg. Wrong type → no valid messages or garbage values.

—-

7. Troubleshooting

SymptomAction
CYCLONEDDS_HOME is not setexport CYCLONEDDS_HOME="$HOME/cyclonedds/install"
No 192.168.123.x on interfaceSet static IP on the port wired to the robot
Ping failedPower robot, check cable/switch
No lowstate in 8sRobot on? unset CYCLONEDDS_URI; verify interface
FSM=1 (Damp)Stand robot (feet on floor); remote L1+UP if paired; re-run check
CheckMode not aiStand robot; wait after power-on; see field guide

—-

8. Deliverable

Submit:
• Log snippet: g1_connection_check.py summary line (PASS or PARTIAL with explanation)
• Your PC interface name and IP on 192.168.123.0/24
• One sentence: what you would do if lowstate never arrives

—-

Next lab: Lab 1 — DDS observation (rt/lowstate) — Subscribe to robot state, print mode_machine and tick, and relate messages to the architecture above.

References

  • • Unitree SDK (Python)
  • • Quick start
  • • Sports / locomotion services
  • • G1 examples (upstream)

labs.title: Subscribe to G1 rt/lowstate
labs.content:
Duration:~30–45 min
Robot motion:None — subscribe only
Prerequisites:Lab 0 connection check PASS on your interface
🎯Learning Objectives
  • Explain the difference between DDS topics (streaming state) and RPC (request/response commands).
  • Subscribe to rt/lowstate with the correct G1 type: LowState_ (unitree_hg).
  • Read tick, mode_machine, mode_pr, and IMU orientation from live messages.
  • Estimate publish rate and know when subscription fails (Lab 0 debugging path).

PDF coverage: Topics & messages · managing sensor data (state streams)

1. Concepts

Pub/sub vs RPC
PatternDirectionG1 exampleUsed for
Topic subscribeRobot → PC (stream)rt/lowstateJoint/IMU state, high rate
Topic publishPC → Robotrt/lowcmdLow-level torque (advanced; not this lab)
RPC clientPC ↔ Robot (call)LocoClient → "sport"Stand, walk, wave (Lab 3+)

Lab 1 uses only pub/sub. You are listening; you are not commanding motion.

Topic and type:
ItemValue
Topic namert/lowstate
IDL packageunitree_hg (G1 / H1-2)
Python typeLowState_ from unitree_sdk2py.idl.unitree_hg.msg.dds_

Go2 uses the same topic name with a different message layout (unitree_go). Wrong type → no messages or garbage.

Top-level LowState_ fields (from SDK):
FieldRole
versionMessage / firmware version bytes
mode_prMode (PR)
mode_machineMachine mode
tickMonotonic counter (use for rate / freshness)
imu_stateOrientation, gyro, accel (rpy, etc.)
motor_statePer-joint state array
wireless_remoteRemote input snapshot

DDS Session (same as Lab 0)

conda activate unitree_env
export CYCLONEDDS_HOME="$HOME/cyclonedds/install"
unset CYCLONEDDS_URI
ChannelFactoryInitialize(0, "en6")  # YOUR NIC — not the robot IP
sub = ChannelSubscriber("rt/lowstate", LowState_)
sub.Init(callback, 10)  # queue depth 10
⚠ Important

Interface name is the PC Ethernet port wired to G1 — it is not 192.168.123.161. Find yours: ip -br addr or ifconfig | grep 192.168.123. Optional: export G1_INTERFACE=en6.

SourceExample name
Linux laptopenp0s31f6, enx207bd22b611a (USB Ethernet)
macOSoften en6

—-

2. Hands-On

Step 1 — Quick readiness:

cd /path/to/repo
python day-05/lab-00/g1_connection_check.py --interface en6 --skip-multicast

Expect PASS (or fix network/DDS per Lab 0).

Step 2 — First message:

python day-05/lab-01/listen_lowstate.py en6 --once

Expected: First rt/lowstate received: with tick, mode_machine, imu_rpy, motors=…

Step 3 — Stream for 15 seconds:

python day-05/lab-01/listen_lowstate.py en6 --duration 15 --rate 1

Note the approximate Hz printed at the end. Typical rates are high (hundreds of Hz); your print rate is throttled by --rate.

Step 4 — Compare with connection check: g1_connection_check.py subscribes to rt/lowstate and also calls LocoClient for FSM. This lab exposes the same data path with readable fields — the split before RPC motion in Lab 3 is intentional.

—-

3. Exercises

Exercise A — Rate: Run with --duration 30 and record message count and Hz. What happens if you unplug Ethernet mid-run?

Exercise B — Field watch: While the robot is standing, note mode_machine and imu_rpy. Gently tilt the torso (if safe and supervised). Do imu_rpy values change?

Exercise C — Pub/sub vs RPC (written): In 2–3 sentences: why does g1_connection_check.py use both ChannelSubscriber("rt/lowstate") and LocoClient for FSM?

Hint

lowstate proves the DDS streaming path is alive; LocoClient reads FSM via the sport RPC service — different channel than the topic alone.

Exercise D — Wrong IDL (discussion): What symptom would you expect if you used unitree_go.LowState_ on G1 rt/lowstate?

Answer

No valid messages, decode errors, or nonsense values — always match IDL package to robot (G1 vs Go2).

—-

4. Troubleshooting

SymptomAction
Interface 'enp0s31f6' does not existUse your NIC from ip -br addr, not a README placeholder
No message in 8s (after Lab 0 PASS)unset CYCLONEDDS_URI; re-run Lab 0
ModuleNotFoundError: unitree_sdk2pyconda activate unitree_env; install SDK
Wrong interface on macOSifconfig | grep 192.168.123 → often en6
CYCLONEDDS_HOME is not setExport before running (see Lab 0)

—-

5. Deliverable

Submit:
• Log snippet: listen_lowstate.py <your-iface> --once (first line)
• Approximate Hz from a 15s run
• Answer to Exercise C

—-

Next lab: Lab 2 — FSM, motion mode & safety — Read FSM id and CheckMode(ai) via RPC without sending motion commands.

References

  • • Lab 0
  • • listen_lowstate.py
  • • Unitree sports services

labs.title: Read-Only FSM Inspection
labs.content:
Duration:~30–45 min
Robot motion:None by default — read-only RPC only
Prerequisites:Lab 1 complete (rt/lowstate streaming)
🎯Learning Objectives
  • Read locomotion FSM id and related sport RPC values without sending motion.
  • Use MotionSwitcherClient.CheckMode() and interpret name='ai'.
  • Relate FSM id to high-level readiness (especially FSM = 1 damp).
  • Document recovery steps before Lab 3 motion commands.

1. Concepts

Two layers of "mode":
LayerAPIWhat it tells you
Motion switcherMotionSwitcherClient.CheckMode()Which sport profile is active (expect ai for high-level SDK)
Loco FSMLocoClient RPC 7001 GET FSM idFinite-state machine phase (stand, damp, walk, …)

Lab 1 proved the DDS topic path (rt/lowstate). Lab 2 uses the RPC path to the "sport" service — same service LocoClient uses in Lab 3, but we only call GET APIs here.

FSM ids you will see (from SDK helpers):
FSM idSDK helperTypical meaning for labs
1Damp()Damp — high-level wave/walk usually blocked
0ZeroTorque()Zero torque
3Sit()Sit
500Start()Start locomotion stack
706Squat2StandUp() / StandUp2Squat()Squat ↔ stand transitions
802(no named helper)Often seen when standing and ready (robot-dependent)

Other ids may appear during transitions. Treat any fsm_id != 1 with CheckMode → ai as a good sign before Lab 3.

RPC API ids (g1_loco_api.py):
IDName
7001GET FSM id
7002GET FSM mode
7003GET balance mode
7004GET swing height
7005GET stand height

The upstream LocoClient exposes Set helpers (SetFsmId, Move, …) but reads use _Call(700x, "{}") — our lab script wraps that pattern.

—-

2. Hands-On

Step 0 — Session setup:

conda activate unitree_env
export CYCLONEDDS_HOME="$HOME/cyclonedds/install"
unset CYCLONEDDS_URI
cd /path/to/repo

Step 1 — Read-only report:

python day-05/lab-02/fsm_readonly.py en6

Expected sections:
1. [DDS topic] — rt/lowstate: OK
2. [Motion switcher RPC] — CheckMode: OK name='ai'
3. [LocoClient RPC] — FSM id, mode, balance, heights
4. Readiness: PASS — ready for Lab 3

Step 2 — Watch FSM during a safe state change (supervised):

With instructor approval, put the robot in damp via remote — then stand it again. In another terminal:

python day-05/lab-02/fsm_readonly.py en6 --watch 30

Observe FSM id change away from 1 when the robot is ready.

Step 3 — Compare with connection check:

python day-05/lab-00/g1_connection_check.py --interface en6 --skip-multicast

Values should match Lab 2's FSM and CheckMode lines.

—-

3. Exercises

Exercise A — Damp vs ready: Record FSM id and readiness line when: 1. Robot is hanging or freshly powered in damp; 2. Robot is standing on the floor with sport mode ai.

Exercise B — lowstate vs FSM: When FSM = 1 (damp), does rt/lowstate still stream? (Yes — topic is independent of FSM.) Run Lab 1 and Lab 2 back-to-back and note both still work.

Exercise C — Recovery plan (written): Write 3 bullet steps you would use if Lab 2 reports FSM=1 before Lab 3. Include when to use the remote vs PC.

Example answer

• Confirm feet on floor and clear space
• Stand robot: remote L1+UP if paired, or wait for automatic stand after power-on
• Re-run fsm_readonly.py until Readiness: PASS (FSM ≠ 1, ai)
• Only then open Lab 3

—-

4. Safety — Recovery Without Lab 3 Motion

This lab script never calls SetFsmId, Move, or Damp. Recovery is manual:

SituationAction
FSM = 1 (damp)Stand robot; avoid Damp() in examples until intentional
CheckMode ≠ aiSee field guide — SelectMode('ai') if needed after low-level use
DDS failsReturn to Lab 0

Optional PC-side recovery (Lab 3 only, clear space): LocoClient().Start() → SetFsmId(500). Not exercised in Lab 2.

—-

5. Troubleshooting

SymptomAction
Readiness: PARTIAL FSM=1Normal in damp; recover per §4
CheckMode failsRobot service / DDS RPC; power-cycle if stuck
lowstate FAILFix Lab 0 network before debugging FSM
FSM id unlike tableLog the value; transitions are OK if readiness is PASS
GET balance/height code=7301Optional RPC on some builds; FSM id + CheckMode are enough

—-

6. Deliverable

Submit:
• Full output of fsm_readonly.py en6 with Readiness: PASS (or PARTIAL + your recovery notes)
• Exercise A table (two FSM ids)
• Exercise C recovery bullets

—-

Next lab: Lab 3 — Interactive FSM & LocoClient actions — Supervised motion: shake hand, read/set FSM — requires Readiness: PASS here.

References

  • • Lab 1
  • • Lab 0
  • • fsm_readonly.py
  • • g1_connection_check.py
  • • Unitree LocoClient API

labs.title: Interactive LocoClient & ShakeHand
labs.content:
Duration:~20–30 min
Robot motion:Yes — this lab sends motion commands; supervision required
Prerequisites:Lab 2 Readiness: PASS (FSM ≠ 1, CheckMode → ai)
🎯Learning Objectives
  • Use the LocoClient API to read and set FSM id on the G1 robot.
  • Run a supervised gesture (ShakeHand) and observe FSM state before and after.
  • Practice safe mode management: understand Damp(), ZeroTorque(), and recovery via SetFsmId.
  • Relate interactive RPC commands to the read-only checks from Labs 0–2.

1. Concepts

This lab demonstrates the difference between telemetry/read-only labs (Labs 1–2) and control (motion) labs. The script provides an interactive menu to execute common LocoClient commands:

Menu idActionEffect
0ZeroTorque()CAUTION — all joints relax; robot may fall instantly
1Damp()Enter damp mode (FSM = 1); blocks high-level wave/walk
2ShakeHand()Supervised gesture — robot moves arm
3GetFsmId()Read current FSM id (same as Lab 2 RPC 7001)
4SetFsmId(id)Set FSM directly (prompts for id: 0, 1, 4, 802, …)

Why this lab matters: Issuing motion commands requires strict safety checks. This lab keeps commands simple and supervised so you can observe effects and learn FSM semantics before Day 6 locomotion sequences.

—-

2. Safety (Required)

⚠ Motion is ENABLED

Clear the floor area (minimum 2 m around the robot). Robot feet on the ground — do not run while hanging. Supervision required for all movements. Keep hands away from the robot during gestures. Never choose option 0 (Zero Torque) unless instructed — the robot will collapse. Avoid option 1 (Damp()) unless you know how to recover (stand + remote or SetFsmId(500)). Be ready to press Ctrl+C to exit the script.

Official example warning: Please ensure there are no obstacles around the robot while running this example.

—-

3. Hands-On

Step 0 — Prepare environment:

conda activate unitree_env
export CYCLONEDDS_HOME="$HOME/cyclonedds/install"
unset CYCLONEDDS_URI
cd /path/to/repo

Step 1 — Confirm readiness:

python day-05/lab-02/fsm_readonly.py en6

Expect Readiness: PASS. Do not proceed if FSM = 1 or CheckMode ≠ ai.

Step 2 — Run the interactive menu:

python day-05/lab-03/setmode_action_fsm.py en6

• Press Enter at the safety prompt.
• Type list to see all menu options.
• Enter a menu id (0–4) or option name to execute.

Suggested sequence (supervised):

1. 3 — GetFsmId (record starting FSM)
2. 2 — ShakeHand (observe gesture; clear space in front)
3. 3 — GetFsmId again (note any change)
4. 4 then enter 500 — SetFsmId to Start (if needed after recovery practice)

Do not run options 0 or 1 unless your instructor directs a damp/recovery exercise.

Step 3 — Verify state after session:

In a second terminal:

python day-05/lab-02/fsm_readonly.py en6

Confirm the robot is still in a safe, recoverable state before leaving the lab.

—-

4. Exercises

Exercise A — FSM before and after: Record FSM id before and after ShakeHand. Did the gesture require a specific FSM state?

Exercise B — Damp recovery (supervised, instructor only): If allowed: run option 1 (Damp), confirm Lab 2 shows FSM=1, then recover using remote stand or SetFsmId(500) via option 4. Document steps.

Exercise C — Compare to Lab 2: Explain in 2–3 sentences why Lab 2 could read FSM without moving the robot, but Lab 3 needs extra safety rules.

—-

5. Troubleshooting

SymptomAction
Script fails to import unitree_sdk2pyActivate unitree_env and ensure the package is installed
No connection / timeoutsVerify interface name and unset CYCLONEDDS_URI
Robot does not move on ShakeHandRe-run Lab 2 — FSM may be 1 (damp); stand robot first
Robot collapsed after Zero TorqueEmergency — power off if needed; do not retry without instructor
Stuck in damp after option 1Stand via remote L1+UP or SetFsmId(500); re-run Lab 2 until PASS

—-

6. Deliverable

Submit:
• A short log or video demonstrating: successful GetFsmId, one supervised ShakeHand, and post-lab Readiness: PASS
• One sentence each: what Damp(), ShakeHand(), and SetFsmId do
• Confirmation that option 0 (Zero Torque) was not used unless instructor-directed

—-

Next camp day: Day 6 — Locomotion & Arm — Scripted stand, wave, walk, and arm actions — requires Readiness: PASS from Lab 2.

References

  • • Lab 2
  • • Lab 1
  • • Lab 0
  • • setmode_action_fsm.py
  • • Unitree sports services

deliverables.label: Connection Check Log
deliverables.description: g1_connection_check.py output with PASS/PARTIAL summary, interface name, and IP

deliverables.label: Lowstate Stream Capture
deliverables.description: listen_lowstate.py output showing tick, mode_machine, IMU, and approximate Hz

deliverables.label: FSM Read-Only Report
deliverables.description: Full fsm_readonly.py output with Readiness: PASS, damp vs ready FSM table, recovery plan

deliverables.label: Supervised ShakeHand Demo
deliverables.description: Log or video of GetFsmId, ShakeHand, and post-lab Readiness: PASS; FSM command descriptions
deliverables.format: Video or terminal log + written answers

06.title: G1 Locomotive & Arm Control Infrastructure
06.thesis: Day 6 bridges high-level action APIs with direct joint SDKs through a disciplined FSM architecture. Students traverse the G1 locomotive state machine — damp → stand → walk → stand → damp — configure stance height, pitch, and roll via JSON-CLI parameters, and learn when to merge direct joint control with high-level Action API calls. Cross-subsystem synchronized execution demands that only one command path is active at any moment, with confirmed stops between every transition.
rules: FSM discipline is non-negotiable: every command must enter through a defined state transition, not a raw joint write. Map the full Damp → Start → Squat/Stand → Move → Stop → Damp cycle before touching any motor. A command issued outside the FSM is a safety violation — treat it as such in code reviews and lab execution.

JSON-CLI stance configuration separates geometry from motion: height, pitch, and roll are set declaratively through structured CLI commands, validated against known-safe ranges, and confirmed via lowstate feedback before any velocity command is issued. Never mix stance configuration with velocity commands in the same message.

Choose the right abstraction per task: LocoClient for high-level locomotion (Move, StopMove, WaveHand), G1ArmActionClient for action_map entries (face_wave, shake_hand, high_five), and rt/arm_sdk for direct joint streaming. The merge point is the FSM — not an ad-hoc interleaving of client calls. Shut down the active client and confirm the stop before switching control surfaces.

pacing.session: G1 Architecture Map — Four Control Surfaces

slides.title: Day 6: G1 Locomotive and Arm Control Infrastructure
slides.thesis: Day 6 bridges high-level action APIs with direct joint SDKs through a disciplined FSM architecture. Students traverse the full control stack — from velocity commands and stance configuration to arm joint mapping and synchronized locomotion-arm execution.

board_data.label: Locomotive Control
board_data.value: FSM state management, high-level controller interaction, low-level motor drivers, velocity commands, and balance transitions.

board_data.label: Arm Control
board_data.value: Low-level joint SDKs merged with high-level Action APIs, joint mapping, kinematic chains, and arm-torso coordination.

board_data.label: JSON-CLI Configuration
board_data.value: Stance height, pitch, roll via structured JSON commands. Precise, repeatable posture adjustments through CLI tools.

board_data.label: Synchronized Execution
board_data.value: Integrated test cases, timing analysis, joint feedback loops, and command latency awareness for safe coordinated movement.

slides.bottom_band: Day 6 introduces gated motion — every command is validated, every transition is confirmed, and every stop is controlled. The FSM observed on Day 5 becomes the FSM commanded on Day 6.

slides.title: Learning Map — Navigating G1 Control
slides.thesis: Initialize FSM → Set Stance → Command Velocity → Monitor Balance → Control Arms → Sync Execution. Control discipline means precision and predictability — every command has a defined effect and a defined verification.
board_data: Initialize FSM: Verify robot is in a known state (damp, stand, or squat_stand) before issuing any commands. Read mode_machine via MotionSwitcherClient before transitioning.
Set Stance: Configure height, pitch, and roll via JSON-CLI to establish the robot baseline posture. The stance determines the CoM starting position for all subsequent motion.
Command Velocity: Send linear (vx, vy) and angular (vyaw) velocity targets through LocoClient. Every velocity command is gated — confirm receipt and state transition before the next command.
Monitor Balance: Continuously read rt/lowstate for IMU orientation, ZMP position, and motor feedback. Balance is active and continuous — a stable robot one second ago may not be stable now.
Control Arms: Merge low-level joint SDKs for precision with high-level Action APIs for convenience. Choose abstraction level per task — fine joint control for calibration, API calls for grasping.
Sync Execution: Validate coordinated locomotion-arm movements with integrated test cases, timing analysis, and data logging. Synchronization failures produce unpredictable behavior.
slides.bottom_band: Control Discipline: Precision and Predictability — Every command flows through the FSM. The FSM is your safety contract: it defines what is allowed in each state and rejects commands that violate the contract.

slides.title: Locomotive Control Subsystems — Introduction
slides.thesis: G1 locomotive control subsystems are responsible for movement and balance. High-level controllers interpret user commands, the mid-level FSM manages operational states, and low-level motor drivers execute physical motion through PID loops with encoder feedback.

board_data.label: High-Level Controller
board_data.value: Receives user commands (move forward, turn, change posture) and translates them into FSM events. Acts as an interpreter between intuitive human intent and structured machine states.

board_data.label: Mid-Level FSM
board_data.value: Manages discrete operational states (Idle, Walk, Stand, Sit) and enforces transition rules. The FSM rejects commands that are invalid for the current state — this is your primary safety gate.

board_data.label: Low-Level Motor Drivers
board_data.value: Directly control joint actuators through PID control loops. Translate FSM outputs into precise electrical signals. Use encoder feedback for closed-loop position and velocity control.

slides.bottom_band: The layered architecture ensures both flexibility and control. High-level commands are interpreted, FSM validates and sequences, motor drivers execute with precision. Each layer has defined responsibilities and failure modes.

slides.title: System Architecture for Movement
slides.thesis: G1 movement is orchestrated through a hierarchical architecture: user commands → high-level controller → mid-level FSM → low-level motor drivers → joint actuators. Each layer adds structure and safety constraints.
board_data: High-Level Controller: Receives user commands (e.g., move forward at 0.5 m/s, turn 90 degrees). Translates intent into structured FSM events with validated parameters.
Mid-Level FSM: Manages states like Idle, Walk, Stand, and Sit. Validates that requested transitions are legal — e.g., refusing a Walk command if the robot is in ZeroTorque state.
Low-Level Motor Drivers: Translate FSM outputs into motor current commands through PID control loops. Execute at 100 Hz–1 kHz for smooth, precise motion with encoder feedback verification.
slides.bottom_band: Flow: User Command → High-Level Controller → FSM Event → State Transition → Motor Driver → Joint Actuator. Every layer validates before passing to the next. No layer trusts the layer above it blindly.

slides.title: Finite State Machine (FSM) State Management
slides.thesis: The FSM is G1's brain for state management: discrete modes of operation with explicit transition rules triggered by events. States, transitions, and events define predictable, auditable behavior.

rows: States
Discrete modes of operation with defined allowed actions.
Idle, Walk, Stand, Sit, Damp, ZeroTorque, SquatStand.
Each state has a known set of permissible commands. Commands outside this set are rejected.
Transitions
Rules for moving between states, each with entry and exit conditions.
Idle → Walk on move command. Walk → Stand on stop command.
Transitions must be atomic and validated. A stuck transition (e.g., lie_to_stand for > 5s) is a safety event.
Events
Triggers for state changes: user input, sensor data, watchdog timeout.
User velocity command, IMU tilt detection, heartbeat loss.
Events are the only way to change state. No event = no transition = robot stays in current state.

slides.bottom_band: The FSM makes G1 behavior predictable and manageable. Every state transition is logged. Every rejected command is a safety signal — investigate why, do not retry blindly.

slides.title: Common G1 FSM States in Action
slides.thesis: Idle: stationary, awaiting commands, minimal power. Walk: actively executing gait pattern. Stand: stationary but maintaining upright, balanced posture. Each state has distinct motor behaviors and safety characteristics.

board_data.label: Idle
board_data.value: Robot is stationary with minimal power consumption. Joints may be in a relaxed or low-torque hold. Awaiting commands. Safe to approach with caution.

board_data.label: Walk
board_data.value: Robot is actively executing a bipedal gait pattern with alternating single and double support phases. ZMP is continuously shifting. Exclusion zone must be maintained.

board_data.label: Stand
board_data.value: Robot maintains an upright, balanced posture with active torque at all leg joints. CoM is centered in support polygon. Most common operational state — verify before commanding.

board_data.label: Sit / SquatStand
board_data.value: Lowered posture with bent knees. Lower CoM increases stability margin. Used as a safe starting position and recovery posture after controlled stops.

slides.bottom_band: Understanding FSM states is prerequisite to commanding G1. You must know what state the robot is in before you can know what commands are valid. Always check mode_machine before any command sequence.

slides.title: High-Level Controller Interaction with FSM
slides.thesis: User commands flow through the high-level controller as an interpreter, converting human-friendly intent into FSM events that trigger structured state transitions. A simple walk command initiates a complex FSM sequence.
board_data: User Command: Operator issues intent (e.g., start walking at 0.5 m/s forward). The command is expressed in human terms — velocity, direction, duration.

High-Level Controller: Translates the command into an FSM event. Validates parameters against current state. If the robot is in Damp, the controller rejects the walk command — Damp → Walk is not a legal transition.

FSM Event Processing: FSM receives the event, checks transition rules, and if valid, initiates the state change. The transition may pass through intermediate states (e.g., Stand → PrepareWalk → Walk).
State Transition Execution: Motor drivers receive updated targets. Balance controller adjusts CoM and ZMP. The robot begins moving — and feedback flows back through the loop.
slides.bottom_band: The high-level controller is the gatekeeper. It validates commands before they reach the FSM. If a command is rejected at the controller level, investigate: is the robot in the right state? Are parameters within limits?

slides.title: Low-Level Motor Drivers — The Muscles of G1
slides.thesis: Motor drivers translate FSM outputs into physical motion through direct actuator control, PID loops, and sensor feedback. This is where digital commands meet physical reality — and where feedback closes the loop.

board_data.label: Direct Actuator Control
board_data.value: Translates FSM target positions/velocities into motor current commands. Each DOF has a dedicated driver receiving individual targets from the FSM output vector.

board_data.label: PID Control Loops
board_data.value: Proportional-Integral-Derivative controllers at each joint ensure precise tracking. P term corrects position error, I term eliminates steady-state offset, D term dampens oscillations.

board_data.label: Closed-Loop Feedback
board_data.value: Encoder data feeds back actual joint position and velocity. The PID controller compares commanded vs. actual and adjusts motor current continuously at 100 Hz–1 kHz.

board_data.label: Torque Monitoring
board_data.value: Motor current is proportional to torque. Unexpected current spikes indicate collisions or joint binding. Torque monitoring is a structural safety loop — exceed threshold, trigger damp.

slides.bottom_band: Motor drivers execute the physical layer. A bug in the FSM produces wrong commands. A bug in the motor driver produces wrong motion. Verify motor feedback matches commanded targets after every command.

slides.title: Velocity Control — Introduction
slides.thesis: Velocity control commands G1 to move at desired speed and direction. Linear velocity governs forward/backward and sideways movement. Angular velocity controls turning (rotation). Both are fundamental to dynamic locomotion.
board_data: Linear Velocity: Forward/backward movement (vx, meters/second) and sideways movement (vy, meters/second). Positive vx = forward. Positive vy = left. Commands are in the robot body frame, not world frame.
Angular Velocity: Turning rate (vyaw, radians/second). Positive vyaw = counter-clockwise rotation when viewed from above. The rotation center is at the robot midpoint between feet.

Command Gating: Every velocity command must include: target velocity, duration or stop condition, and a confirmation check. Never send an unbounded velocity command — always define when and how it stops.
Balance Interaction: Velocity changes shift the ZMP. The balance controller must compensate. Sudden velocity changes (high acceleration) can push the ZMP outside the support polygon.
slides.bottom_band: Velocity control is how we make G1 move. But velocity is not position — the robot will keep moving until commanded to stop. Always pair a velocity command with a stop condition.

slides.title: Implementing Linear Velocity Commands
slides.thesis: Linear velocity commands specify forward/backward (vx) and sideways (vy) speeds in meters per second. Send via JSON-CLI or LocoClient API with structured key-value pairs. Always verify receipt with lowstate feedback.

rows: Forward Velocity
linear_x
g1_cmd_vel --linear_x 0.5
G1 moves forward at 0.5 m/s in its body frame.
Sideways Velocity
linear_y
g1_cmd_vel --linear_y 0.3
G1 moves left at 0.3 m/s. Negative = right.
Combined Linear
linear_x, linear_y
g1_cmd_vel --linear_x 0.3 --linear_y 0.2
G1 moves diagonally forward-left. Vectors are additive.
Stop Command
linear_x: 0, linear_y: 0
g1_cmd_vel --linear_x 0 --linear_y 0
G1 decelerates to zero linear velocity via controlled stop profile.

slides.bottom_band: JSON structure: {"linear_x": 0.5, "linear_y": 0.0}. Always include both x and y components even if one is zero — omitting a component may retain a previous value.

slides.title: Implementing Angular Velocity Commands
slides.thesis: Angular velocity commands control G1 rotation about the vertical (z) axis in radians per second. Positive vyaw = counter-clockwise. Combine with linear velocity for curved paths and turning maneuvers.

rows: Angular Velocity
angular_z
g1_cmd_vel --angular_z 0.2
G1 rotates counter-clockwise at 0.2 rad/s (~11.5 deg/s).
Sharp Turn
angular_z = 0.5
g1_cmd_vel --angular_z 0.5
G1 rotates at ~28.6 deg/s. Higher values require more balance compensation.
Combined Motion
linear_x + angular_z
g1_cmd_vel --linear_x 0.3 --angular_z 0.2
G1 walks forward while turning — a curved trajectory. Balance controller must handle both simultaneously.
Zero Rotation
angular_z: 0
g1_cmd_vel --angular_z 0
G1 maintains current heading. Always include angular_z to prevent unintended rotation from prior commands.

slides.bottom_band: JSON structure: {"angular_z": 0.2}. Angular velocity is in radians/second. 1 radian ≈ 57.3 degrees. A full 360° turn at 0.2 rad/s takes ~31 seconds — plan your commands accordingly.

slides.title: Managing Balance States During Transitions
slides.thesis: Balance is a continuous, active process especially during movement transitions. G1 employs dynamic balance control with CoM adjustment and adaptive foot placement to prevent falls when starting, stopping, or changing direction.

board_data.label: Dynamic Balance
board_data.value: Active control that continuously adjusts joint torques to keep the ZMP within the support polygon. Not a static property — balance is actively maintained at every control cycle (100 Hz).

board_data.label: CoM Adjustment
board_data.value: Shifting body weight by adjusting hip, knee, and ankle angles. Before initiating a step, G1 shifts CoM over the stance foot to prepare for single support phase.

board_data.label: Foot Placement Strategy
board_data.value: Adapting step location based on current velocity and balance state. If the robot is tilting right, the next footstep shifts right to catch the fall and restore equilibrium.

board_data.label: Transition Vulnerability
board_data.value: The highest risk of falling occurs during state transitions (Stand → Walk, Walk → Stop). The support polygon changes size, and the ZMP must be actively managed through the transition.

slides.bottom_band: Never interrupt a transition with another command. Wait for the robot to reach a stable state (verify via lowstate mode_machine) before sending the next command. Interrupting a transition is the #1 cause of balance loss.

slides.title: Stance Configurations via JSON-CLI — Introduction
slides.thesis: Stance defines G1's base posture: height, body pitch, and body roll. JSON-CLI provides a structured, human-readable, machine-parseable format for precise and repeatable posture adjustments.
board_data: Stance Definition: The robot's base posture including overall height (leg extension), body pitch (forward/backward lean), and body roll (sideways lean). Determines the starting CoM position for all motion.
JSON-CLI Interface: Command-line tool accepting JSON-formatted key-value pairs. Human-readable for debugging, machine-parseable for automation. Same format across all G1 configuration commands.
Precision Benefits: Numeric parameters in standard units (meters, radians) enable exact, repeatable posture adjustments. No guesswork — a height of 0.8 m means exactly 0.8 m, every time.
Safety Check: Always verify the current stance before setting a new one. Read current joint positions from lowstate motor_state to understand the starting configuration before commanding changes.
slides.bottom_band: JSON-CLI stance configuration is your first active (non-read-only) G1 command. Every stance command changes the robot's physical configuration. Verify receipt, verify execution, verify stability — before the next command.

slides.title: Configuring Stance Height
slides.thesis: Stance height adjusts G1's overall leg extension in meters. Lower height = lower CoM = more stable but less reach. Higher height = higher CoM = less stable but greater workspace and visibility.

rows: Stance Height
stance_height
g1_set_stance --height 0.8
G1 adjusts leg extension to achieve 0.8 m overall height. Lower = more stable.
Low Height (0.6 m)
stance_height: 0.6
g1_set_stance --height 0.6
Deep squat: maximum stability, reduced workspace. Use for learning and recovery.
Standard Height (0.8 m)
stance_height: 0.8
g1_set_stance --height 0.8
Default operational height balancing stability and reach. Most labs start here.
Extended Height (1.0 m)
stance_height: 1.0
g1_set_stance --height 1.0
Near-full extension: maximum reach, reduced stability margin. Use only when task requires it.

slides.bottom_band: Height changes shift the CoM vertically. The balance controller compensates, but rapid height changes can cause oscillation. Use incremental adjustments (0.05 m steps) when testing new heights.

slides.title: Configuring Stance Pitch and Roll
slides.thesis: Pitch controls forward/backward body lean. Roll controls sideways body lean. Both in radians. These parameters fine-tune balance for specific tasks — pitch forward to reach, roll to shift weight for single-leg stands.

rows: Body Pitch
stance_pitch
Positive = lean forward (reach). Negative = lean backward (look up). Affects CoM projection on ground.
Body Roll
stance_roll
Positive = lean right. Negative = lean left. Used for weight shifts during stepping.
Combined Lean
pitch + roll
see ranges above
g1_set_stance --pitch 0.1 --roll -0.05: G1 leans forward and slightly left simultaneously.
Zero Orientation
pitch: 0, roll: 0
default
g1_set_stance --pitch 0 --roll 0: Returns G1 to neutral upright posture. Always a safe fallback.

slides.bottom_band: Pitch and roll are in radians. Small values have large effects: 0.1 rad ≈ 5.7° of lean. Always start with small values (0.05 rad) and increment gradually. Excessive lean shifts the ZMP close to the support polygon edge.

slides.title: JSON-CLI Structures for Stance Configuration
slides.thesis: Combine height, pitch, and roll into a single JSON-CLI command for unified posture control. The combined format is flexible, readable, and enables setting up specific initial conditions for experiments or tasks.
board_data: Combined Command Format: g1_set_stance --config '{"height": 0.7, "pitch": 0.05, "roll": 0.0}' — Adjusts multiple stance parameters in a single atomic command. All parameters take effect simultaneously.
Flexibility: Any subset of parameters can be included. Omitted parameters retain their current values. This enables incremental adjustments — change only height while keeping pitch and roll constant.
Human-Readable: JSON key-value pairs are self-documenting. A glance at the command shows exactly what will change. This readability is a safety feature — ambiguous commands cause accidents.
Machine-Parseable: JSON can be generated programmatically from scripts, configuration files, or sensor data. Automate stance configuration based on task requirements or real-time feedback.
slides.bottom_band: Combined Command Example: g1_set_stance --config '{"height": 0.7, "pitch": 0.05, "roll": 0.0}'. Always verify the new stance via lowstate motor_state after sending. Compare commanded vs. actual joint positions.

slides.title: Arm Control Infrastructure — Introduction
slides.thesis: G1 arm control enables object manipulation and environmental interaction through a dual approach: low-level joint SDKs for precision and high-level Action APIs for complex pre-defined movements.

board_data.label: Arm Control Goal
board_data.value: Enable G1 to manipulate objects, interact with the environment, and perform dexterous tasks. Arms add 10–14 DOF (5–7 per arm) to the robot's capabilities.

board_data.label: Low-Level Joint SDK
board_data.value: Direct motor access: control individual joint angles, velocities, and torques. Essential for calibration, fine adjustments, and custom movement algorithms.

board_data.label: High-Level Action API
board_data.value: Pre-defined complex movements (reach, grasp, wave) that abstract away joint kinematics. Faster development for common tasks — call one function instead of coordinating 5+ joints.

board_data.label: Hybrid Approach
board_data.value: Combine precision of SDK with convenience of APIs. Use API for broad movement, SDK for fine-tuning. Choose the right abstraction level per task.

slides.bottom_band: Arm control is where G1 transitions from a walking platform to a manipulation platform. The dual SDK/API approach gives you both surgical precision and rapid development — learn when to use each.

slides.title: Fine-Grained Control: Low-Level Joint SDKs
slides.thesis: Low-level Joint Configuration SDKs provide direct motor access for controlling individual joint angles, velocities, and torques. Essential for calibration, fine adjustments, and custom movement development.
board_data: Direct Motor Access: Control individual joints by ID (e.g., shoulder_pitch, elbow_roll) with set_joint_angle(joint_id, angle). Each joint is independently addressable.
High Precision: Specify joint angles in radians, velocities in rad/s, and torques in Nm. Precision enables calibration routines, custom trajectory generation, and research-grade motion control.
SDK Functions: set_joint_angle(joint_id, angle_rad), set_joint_velocity(joint_id, vel_rad_s), get_joint_torque(joint_id), get_joint_position(joint_id). Read and write individual joints.
Safety Constraint: Every joint command must include joint limit validation. Check commanded angle against known mechanical limits before sending. A command beyond limits can damage the gearbox.
slides.bottom_band: Low-level SDK = surgical precision. Use when you need exact control over a specific joint. The trade-off: you are responsible for kinematics, collision avoidance, and joint limit enforcement.

slides.title: Simplified Actions: High-Level Action APIs
slides.thesis: High-level Action APIs offer pre-defined complex movements that abstract away joint kinematics and trajectory generation. Faster development for common tasks at the cost of reduced fine-grained control.

rows: Pre-defined Movements
Complex motions packaged as single function calls.
reach_for_object(object_id), wave_hand(), grasp(object_id).
Common tasks where the exact joint trajectory does not matter.
Kinematic Abstraction
Hides inverse kinematics, trajectory generation, and joint coordination.
Call reach_to(x, y, z) instead of computing 5+ joint angles manually.
Rapid prototyping and standard interaction patterns.
Ease of Use
Faster development cycle — one API call replaces dozens of SDK calls.
API: grasp(). SDK equivalent: 5+ set_joint_angle() calls with timing coordination.
When development speed matters more than trajectory customization.
Built-in Safety
APIs include pre-validated joint limits and collision checks.
reach_for_object() verifies target is within workspace before moving.
When you want the SDK safety guarantees without implementing them yourself.

slides.bottom_band: High-Level API = rapid development with built-in safety. Use for standard manipulation tasks. The trade-off: you cannot customize the trajectory — the API chooses the path to the target.

slides.title: Merging SDKs with Action APIs — The Bridge
slides.thesis: The real power comes from merging both approaches. Action APIs are built on top of SDK functions. This hybrid strategy gives developers the flexibility to choose the right control level for each task — broad gestures or minute adjustments.
board_data: Hybrid Architecture: Action APIs are implemented using SDK functions internally. Calling reach_to(x, y, z) computes inverse kinematics, validates joint limits, and calls set_joint_angle() for each DOF.

Layered Control: SDK layer → Action API layer → Application layer. Your code can call APIs directly, call SDK functions directly, or mix both in the same script. No artificial restriction on which layer you use.
Flexibility: Use API for the broad movement (reach to object), then SDK for fine-tuning (adjust grip force by 5%). Use SDK for custom research trajectories, API for standard lab exercises.

Integration Pattern: 1) API call for high-level action. 2) Read joint feedback via SDK. 3) SDK call for fine adjustment. 4) API call for next high-level action. Repeat — hybrid scripts are the norm, not the exception.
slides.bottom_band: The SDK is always available beneath the API. If an API does not give you the control you need, drop down to the SDK layer for that specific step, then return to the API layer for the next. This is the professional pattern.

slides.title: Understanding G1 Joint Mapping
slides.thesis: Effective arm control requires understanding the kinematic chain — the sequence of joints and links forming each arm — and the unique IDs for each motor. The joint mapping determines which ID to target for each desired movement.

board_data.label: Kinematic Chain
board_data.value: The ordered sequence of joints and links from shoulder to end-effector. Each arm typically has 5–7 DOF arranged as shoulder (3 DOF) → elbow (1–2 DOF) → wrist (1–2 DOF).

board_data.label: Joint IDs
board_data.value: Unique string identifiers for each motor: shoulder_pitch, shoulder_roll, shoulder_yaw, elbow_pitch, elbow_roll, wrist_pitch, wrist_roll. Use IDs when calling SDK joint functions.

board_data.label: Degrees of Freedom
board_data.value: Arm5 config = 5 DOF per arm (10 total). Arm7 config = 7 DOF per arm (14 total). More DOF = more dexterity but more complex coordination and larger command arrays.

board_data.label: Workspace Limits
board_data.value: Each arm has a defined workspace — the 3D volume it can reach. Commands outside this workspace are rejected. Know the arm reach envelope before planning manipulation tasks.

slides.bottom_band: Joint mapping is your arm control reference. Always confirm the DOF variant (arm5 vs arm7) before writing arm control code. The wrong joint count produces wrong-dimension command arrays — a safety hazard.

slides.title: Coordinating Arm-Torso Movements
slides.thesis: Arms do not operate in isolation. Coordinating arm movements with torso adjustments enhances reach, improves balance, and makes interactions more natural. Integrated commands must be sent to both arm and torso controllers.
board_data: Enhanced Reach: Torso lean extends the arm workspace. A forward pitch of 0.1 rad can add 10+ cm of forward reach. Combine torso pitch with arm extension for maximum reach distance.
Improved Balance: Extending an arm shifts the CoM. A torso counter-lean compensates — if the right arm reaches forward, the torso leans slightly back and left to keep the ZMP centered.

Integrated Commands: Send arm and torso commands together, not sequentially. Sequential commands cause the robot to adjust balance twice — first for the arm, then for the torso — increasing risk of oscillation.
Coordination Timing: Arm and torso commands must execute within the same control cycle. Verify that both subsystems receive and acknowledge commands before the next control cycle.
slides.bottom_band: Full-body coordination is the difference between a robot that moves and a robot that moves gracefully. Practice arm-torso coordination with small, slow movements first — build confidence before attempting dynamic reaching.

slides.title: SDK and API Integration — Practical Workflows
slides.thesis: Real-world applications use hybrid scripts combining SDK precision with API convenience. Choose based on task complexity: API for broad actions, SDK for fine control. The decision tree guides which abstraction to use.

rows: Standard Grasp
Action API
grasp_object(target_id)
Pre-defined trajectory with built-in force control. No need to coordinate individual fingers.
Custom Trajectory
Joint SDK
set_joint_angle() for each DOF along a computed path.
Research-grade control requires exact joint trajectories that APIs cannot produce.
Calibration Routine
Joint SDK
Move each joint to limit, record encoder value, compute offset.
Calibration requires individual joint control — APIs operate on groups of joints.
Hybrid Task
API + SDK
API: reach_to(x,y,z). SDK: adjust_wrist_angle(+0.05). API: grasp().
API handles the broad motion. SDK fine-tunes the final approach. Best of both worlds.

slides.bottom_band: Decision rule: If the task has a standard API call, use it. If you need joint-level precision or a non-standard trajectory, use the SDK. If you need both — and most real tasks do — use both in the same script.

slides.title: Sending JSON-Based Commands
slides.thesis: JSON is the standard format for structured command data exchange with G1. Commands use key-value pairs for actions and parameters, delivered via CLI or API endpoint. JSON readability is a safety feature.
board_data: Standard Format: JSON (JavaScript Object Notation) is widely used for structured data. G1 accepts JSON-formatted commands for locomotion, arm actions, stance configuration, and parameter setting.

Command Structure: Key-value pairs specify action type and parameters. Example: {"action": "move", "linear_x": 0.5, "duration_s": 3.0}. Every command has a defined schema — validate against it before sending.
Delivery Methods: CLI tools (g1_cmd_vel, g1_set_stance) for interactive use. API endpoints for programmatic control. Both accept the same JSON structure — learn the format once, use it everywhere.

Validation: Every JSON command must be validated: are all required keys present? Are values within acceptable ranges? Is the robot in a state that accepts this command? Validate before sending — never assume.
slides.bottom_band: JSON readability is a safety feature. A glance at {"linear_x": 0.5, "angular_z": 0.2} tells you exactly what the robot will do. Opaque binary commands hide intent. JSON commands reveal it.

slides.title: Reading Joint Feedback for Arms
slides.thesis: Joint feedback provides real-time data on arm joint angles, velocities, and torques via DDS topics. Monitor with dds_msg_echo or custom subscribers. Feedback closes the loop — it proves your command was executed correctly.

board_data.label: Real-Time Data
board_data.value: Joint angles (rad), velocities (rad/s), and torques (Nm) streamed continuously. Compare commanded vs. actual values to detect tracking errors or mechanical issues.

board_data.label: DDS Topics
board_data.value: Arm joint feedback is published on dedicated DDS topics. Subscribe with ChannelSubscriber to receive live data. Same pattern as rt/lowstate — consistent across all G1 subsystems.

board_data.label: Monitoring Tools
board_data.value: dds_msg_echo for quick terminal inspection. Custom subscribers for programmatic monitoring with logging, alerting, and automated validation checks.

board_data.label: Feedback Loop
board_data.value: Command → Execute → Measure → Compare → Adjust. If actual position deviates from commanded by > threshold, investigate: joint binding, collision, or calibration error.

slides.bottom_band: Feedback proves execution. Without feedback, you are assuming your command worked — and in robotics, assumptions cause accidents. Always read joint feedback after every arm command.

slides.title: Validating Synchronized Locomotive-Arm Execution
slides.thesis: For complex tasks, locomotion and arm movements must be perfectly synchronized. Integrated test cases, timing analysis, visual inspection, and data logging validate that actions occur in the correct sequence and within tolerances.
board_data: Integrated Test Cases: Design tests involving both locomotion and arm tasks — e.g., walk to a location, then reach for an object. Test the full sequence, not just individual components.

Timing Analysis: Measure when each action starts and ends. Ensure the arm does not begin reaching before locomotion stops. Sequence errors produce unpredictable behavior — the arm reaches for where the object was, not where it is.
Visual Inspection: Watch the robot during synchronized tests. Does the movement look smooth? Are there hesitations or jerks? Visual inspection catches issues that data logs miss — trust your eyes.

Data Logging: Record timestamps, commanded values, and actual feedback for every action in the sequence. Post-hoc analysis reveals timing gaps, synchronization failures, and unexpected interactions between subsystems.
slides.bottom_band: Synchronized execution is the hardest control challenge on Day 6. Start with sequential actions (stop locomotion → then move arm) before attempting simultaneous actions (walk while reaching).

slides.title: Control Discipline — Best Practices
slides.thesis: Control discipline means clear intent, incremental testing, parameter validation, and emergency stop readiness. These habits minimize errors and enhance safety — they are the difference between professional operation and guesswork.

board_data.label: Clear Intent
board_data.value: Define precise goals for each command: what should happen, in what time frame, with what verification. Vague commands produce vague results. Specific commands produce auditable outcomes.

board_data.label: Incremental Testing
board_data.value: Test small changes before integrating larger ones. A 0.1 m/s velocity change that works does not guarantee a 0.5 m/s change will work. Scale up gradually with verification at each step.

board_data.label: Parameter Validation
board_data.value: Ensure every commanded value is within safe operating limits. Check joint angles against mechanical limits. Check velocity against stability margins. Validate before sending — never assume parameters are safe.

board_data.label: E-Stop Readiness
board_data.value: Always be prepared to halt operation. Know where the E-stop is. Know the controlled stop procedure. If something looks wrong, stop first, investigate second. Hesitation during an anomaly amplifies damage.

slides.bottom_band: Control discipline is not about following rules — it is about building habits that prevent errors before they happen. A disciplined operator stops at the first sign of unexpected behavior. An undisciplined operator waits to see what happens.

slides.title: Joint Validation — Ensuring Accuracy
slides.thesis: Joint validation verifies that commanded movements execute correctly: compare commanded vs. actual positions, monitor torques for anomalies, check range of motion, and verify repeatability across multiple executions.

rows: Position Verification
Commanded vs. actual joint angles match.
Read encoder feedback after command. Compute absolute error = |commanded − actual|.
Error < 0.01 rad (0.6°) for static positions. Error < 0.05 rad during motion.
Torque Monitoring
Unexpected forces or collisions.
Monitor motor current (proportional to torque). Compare to expected torque for the commanded motion.
Torque within 150% of expected. Sustained spike > 150% = collision or binding.
Range of Motion
Joints operate within physical limits.
Command joint to min and max angles. Verify encoder reads within documented limits.
No mechanical stop contact. Encoder reads smoothly across full range.
Repeatability
Consistent movement over multiple executions.
Command the same motion 5 times. Record actual positions. Compute standard deviation.
Standard deviation < 0.005 rad across 5 trials. Larger variance = mechanical or calibration issue.

slides.bottom_band: Joint validation is your quality assurance. A joint that moves to 0.49 rad when commanded to 0.50 rad has a 0.01 rad error — is that acceptable for your task? Define your accuracy requirements before testing.

slides.title: Command Latency Awareness
slides.thesis: Command latency is the delay between sending a command and G1's response. Sources include network delays, processing time, and actuator response. Latency affects responsiveness and synchronization — mitigate through optimized communication.

board_data.label: Latency Sources
board_data.value: Network transmission (UDP packet time), DDS serialization/deserialization, FSM processing (event queue), motor driver PID response, and actuator mechanical inertia. Each adds milliseconds.

board_data.label: Typical Values
board_data.value: Network: < 1 ms on direct Ethernet. DDS: < 0.5 ms. FSM: 1–5 ms. Motor driver: 1–10 ms. Total: 3–17 ms from command send to motion start under normal conditions.

board_data.label: Impact on Control
board_data.value: Latency creates a gap between commanded state and actual state. At 0.5 m/s, 17 ms latency = 8.5 mm position uncertainty. For precision tasks, latency matters — measure it, do not ignore it.

board_data.label: Mitigation Strategies
board_data.value: Use direct Ethernet (not Wi-Fi). Minimize DDS history depth. Keep callbacks fast. Avoid blocking operations in control loops. Use real-time priority threads where available.

slides.bottom_band: Latency cannot be eliminated — only managed. Know your system latency and account for it in your control strategy. A 17 ms delay is negligible for walking but critical for catching a falling object.

labs.title: DOF Variant Confirmation & Motion Readiness
labs.content:
Confirm the G1 DOF variant (arm5 vs arm7) and re-verify Day 5 readiness before any Day 6 motion.

- Re-run Day 5 readiness gate (6 checkpoints)
- Subscribe to rt/lowstate and count motor_states
- Classify variant: ≤25 motors = arm5 (23 DOF), >25 motors = arm7 (29 DOF)
- Record variant in lab record; require operator verbal confirmation
- Reconfirm FSM state (must be non-damp, stable, ideally 706 squat/stand)
- Confirm CheckMode returns single-owner with rc=0
- Brief the Day 6 safety contract (7 rules) before any motion command

code_files.name: g1_dof_variant_check.py
code_files.code:
"""G1 DOF Variant Check — confirm arm5 vs arm7 before any motion."""

labs.title: High-Level Locomotion — LocoClient
labs.content:
Execute gated, sequential LocoClient commands starting with WaveHand and progressing to posture and short translation.

- Initialize LocoClient with confirmed interface
- Sequence: WaveHand → observe → StopMove → confirm stop → HighStand → observe → LowStand → observe
- Then (instructor approval): Move(0.05, 0, 0) for 2 s → StopMove → confirm velocity ≈ 0 in lowstate
- Document every command with return code and observed behavior
- Practice abort: spotter calls unexpected-motion → operator immediately Damp()
- Never chain commands without confirmed intermediate stops

labs.title: High-Level Arm Actions — G1ArmActionClient
labs.content:
Execute G1ArmActionClient action_map entries with the same gating discipline as locomotion.

- Initialize G1ArmActionClient after confirming locomotion is stopped
- Sequence: face wave → release arm → shake hand → release arm → high five → release arm
- Always call release arm between different actions to return to safe neutral posture
- Confirm arm reach envelope is clear of people and objects before each action
- Document each action with return code and observed arm movement
- After arm actions complete, return to LowStand or safe posture
